elements.sent-2411-463: In \cite{kim:2022aa , it is shown the pion mass is $\theta$ dependent and influence the nucleon mass $m_\mathrm{N}$ through \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\frac{\partial \ln m_N}{\partial \ln m_\pi^2} \approx 0.06 \, , \frac{\partial \ln m_\pi^2}{\partial \left(\theta^2\right)} = -\frac{m_u m_d}{2(m_u+m_d)^2}=-0.109 \, \Rightarrow \frac{\partial \ln m_N}{\partial \left(\theta^2\right)} \approx -0.065 \, ,\end{equation} \end{subequations such that, for an atom made of (N+Z) nucleons, the contribution of the nucleons rest mass to the atom rest mass to the axionic charge is given by \begin{equation}[Q^\mathrm{atom}_M]_a\Big|_\mathrm{const.} = \frac{\partial \ln m_N}{\partial \left(\theta^2\right)} \approx -0.065 \, , \label{axionic_charge_rest}\end{equation} meaning that it is independent of the number of nucleons inside the atom, or in other words of the atomic species.}}}
elements.sent-2411-464: Let us now focus on the contribution of the binding energy of the nuclei to the axionic mass charge and show that it is composition dependent.
elements.sent-2411-465: As computed in \cite{damour:2010zr , the binding energy of the nuclei depends to first order on the mass of the pions and therefore, following Eq.~\eqref{pions_axions_dep , on the ALP.}}
elements.sent-2411-466: We will now use the results from Section IV of \cite{damour:2010zr to infer the analytical expression of the dependency of the binding energy to the ALP and to the mass number $A$ and the charge number $Z$.}
elements.sent-2411-467: Four different interactions contribute to the binding energy \cite{damour:2010zr : the central force $E_{\mathrm{central }$ coming from the isospin symmetric central nuclear force, the asymmetry energy $E_{\mathrm{asym }$, i.e the residual energy from the asymmetry between neutrons and protons inside the nucleus, the Coulomb force $E_{\mathrm{Coulomb }$ depending on how tightly the nucleons are packed together and the pairing energy $E_{\mathrm{pairing }$ leading to \begin{equation}E_bind = E_{central}+E_{asym}\frac{(A-2Z)^2}{A}+E_{Coulomb}\frac{Z(Z-1)}{A^{1/3}} -\delta \frac{E_{pairing}}{A^{1/2}} \, ,\end{equation} where $\delta = \pm 1$ respectively for even-even and odd-odd number of protons-neutrons in the nuclei, and $\delta = 0$ otherwise.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-468: The dominant interactions are an attractive scalar $\eta_S$ and a repulsive vector $\eta_V$, and \cite{damour:2010zr shows that the former is more sensitive to the pion mass, implying in our case \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\frac{\partial E_Central}{\partial (\theta^2)}\approx \frac{\partial E_Central}{\partial \eta_S} \frac{\partial \eta_S}{\partial \ln m^2_\pi}\frac{\partial \ln m^2_\pi}{\partial (\theta^2)} \, ,\end{equation} with \cite{Introductory_Nucl_Phys, damour:2010zr \begin{equation}E_Central \approx -(120A-97A^{2/3})\eta_S\, , \frac{\partial \eta_S}{\partial \ln m^2_\pi} = -0.35  \ MeV \, .\end{equation}. \end{subequations.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-512: Search for axions with an optical cavity and an optical fiber Usually, cavities can be used to search for axion-photon coupling $g_{a\gamma}$ through the axion-photon conversion under a strong magnetic field.
elements.sent-2411-513: This is the case for ADMX \cite{ADMX_2002,ADMX:2010,ADMX:2018_1,ADMX:2019,ADMX:2021 or CAPP \cite{Capp:2020_1, Capp:2020_2, CAPP:2020_3 experiments.}}
elements.sent-2411-514: In practice, these experiments aim at detecting photons sourced by this strong magnetic field and the DM axion field through the inverse Primakoff effect.
elements.sent-2411-515: Then, the photons would resonate inside the cavity in order to increase drastically the signal.
elements.sent-2411-516: In this case, the frequency of the produced photons correspond to the axion mass, and therefore these experiments are sensitive to a very narrow mass interval (which is essentially the width of the resonant frequency of the cavity).
elements.sent-2411-517: Cavities can also search for this $g_{a\gamma}$ coupling through the effect derived in Section \ref{axion_photon_coupling , i.e the phase velocity difference between the left and right polarization of light.}
elements.sent-2411-518: In the following, we will consider an optical cavity to compute the phase observable.
elements.sent-2411-519: Then, in Chapter ~\ref{chap:sens_experiments , we will make some estimation on the sensitivity of the optical cavity used in the DAMNED experiment \cite{Savalle21 .}}
elements.sent-2411-520: We will show that unfortunately, such experiments are not competitive for the search of the axion-photon coupling.
elements.sent-2411-521: Birefringence and oscillation of the length of the cavity We consider a setup where two optical cavities are used : one in which a linear polarized light is used, i.e the phase velocity is exactly the speed of light $c$, and another where we send a right polarized light.
elements.sent-2411-522: This could be done, e.g, by inserting a quarter wave plate in front of one of the cavities which would change a linear polarization to a circular one.
elements.sent-2411-523: Then, we measure the phase difference between the output of the two cavities.
elements.sent-2411-524: From Eq.~\eqref{delta_c_axion_photon , the left and right circular polarized light respectively evolve with phase velocity \begin{subequations \begin{equation}c \left(1 - \frac{E_P}{c^2} \frac{\sqrt{4\pi G \rho_DM}g_{a\gamma}}{k}\sin(\omega_a t + \Phi)\right) \equiv c - \delta c \sin(\omega_a t+\Phi)\, c \left(1 + \frac{E_P}{c^2} \frac{\sqrt{4\pi G \rho_DM}g_{a\gamma}}{k}\sin(\omega_a t + \Phi)\right) \equiv c + \delta c \sin(\omega_a t+\Phi)\, ,\end{equation} with $\omega_a$ the oscillation frequency of the axion field, $E_P$ the reduced Planck energy and \begin{equation}\label{delta_c_axion_photon_right} \delta c = \frac{\sqrt{4\pi G \rho_DM} E_P g_{a\gamma}}{\omega_0} \, ,\end{equation} \end{subequations with $\omega_0 = k c$, the unperturbed light angular frequency.}}}
elements.formula-2411-132: \begin{equation}c \left(1 - \frac{E_P}{c^2} \frac{\sqrt{4\pi G \rho_DM}g_{a\gamma}}{k}\sin(\omega_a t + \Phi)\right) \equiv c - \delta c \sin(\omega_a t+\Phi)\, c \left(1 + \frac{E_P}{c^2} \frac{\sqrt{4\pi G \rho_DM}g_{a\gamma}}{k}\sin(\omega_a t + \Phi)\right) \equiv c + \delta c \sin(\omega_a t+\Phi)\, ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-525: In this model, the cavity length $\ell$ is unchanged, but is directly related to the light travel time $\tau_\leftrightarrow(t)$ inside the cavity as \begin{equation}2 \ell = \int_0^{\tau_\leftrightarrow(t)}c(t')dt' \, ,\end{equation} where $c(t)$ is the time dependent phase velocity of light whose exact form depends on the light polarization Eq.~\eqref{delta_c_axion_photon .}
elements.sent-2411-526: Assuming a right handed photon starting its propagation at the left mirror at time $t-\tau_\leftrightarrow(t)$, it will come back to the same spatial point at time t, where the travel time is given by \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\label{time delay} \tau_\leftrightarrow(t) = \frac{2\ell}{c} - \int_{t-\tau_\leftarrow}^t \frac{\delta c_\leftarrow(t')}{c}dt' - \int_{t-\tau_\leftarrow-\tau_\rightarrow}^{t-\tau_\leftarrow} \frac{\delta c_\rightarrow(t')}{c}dt' \,,\end{equation} where $\tau_\rightarrow$ and $\tau_\leftarrow$ are the time intervals the photon takes on the trip there and back respectively and \begin{equation}\delta c_\rightarrow(t) = +\delta c \sin(\omega_at+\Phi) \delta c_\leftarrow(t) = -\delta c \sin(\omega_at+\Phi) \, ,\end{equation} \end{subequations respectively for right and left handed polarizations, since the photon has opposite circular polarization on the way there and back due to the mirror reflection.}}
elements.sent-2411-527: Indeed, the mirror reverses one of the linear polarizations while keeping the other unchanged, which implies that a circular polarization changes other (up to an irrelevant phase) We can then interpret this oscillation of phase velocity of light as an oscillation of the cavity length itself, i.e we can write \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\label{eq:tau_ell} \tau_\leftrightarrow(t) = \frac{2(\ell + \delta\ell(t))}{c} \, ,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\label{eq:delta-ell} \delta\ell(t) = \delta c f(t) \,,\end{equation} with \begin{equation}\label{eq:f(t)} f(t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[\int_{t-\tau_\leftarrow}^t \sin(\omega_at'+\Phi) dt' - \int_{t-\tau_\leftarrow-\tau_\rightarrow}^{t-\tau_\leftarrow} \sin(\omega_at'+\Phi)dt' \right] \,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-528: Phase shift at the output of the cavity In order to obtain the phase shift of the electric field resulting from the length oscillation Eq.~\eqref{delta_l , we will sum over a large number $N$ of field contributions that are reflected back and forth in the cavity (i.e $N \rightarrow \infty$).}
elements.sent-2411-529: The calculation is explicitly presented in Appendix ~\ref{ap:phase_shift_cavity .}
elements.sent-2411-530: Here, we immediately show the amplitude of the phase shift (oscillating at the axion frequency $\omega_a$) between the transmitted field and the input field as \begin{equation}|\Delta \phi(t)| \approx \frac{2\sqrt{16\pi G \rho_DM} E_P g_{a\gamma}}{\omega_a c\sqrt{1-2r^2\cos\left(\frac{2\omega_a \ell}{c}\right)+r^4}}\sin^2\left(\frac{\omega_a \ell}{2c}\right) \label{eq:phase_final_DAMNED}\, ,\end{equation} where $r$ is the mirror reflectivity of the cavity.
elements.sent-2411-531: This means the phase observable is independent of the laser frequency.
elements.sent-2411-532: When the axion frequency corresponds to an even mode of the cavity, $\omega_a \ell/c = (2n +1) \pi$, and considering that $r = 1- \epsilon$, $\epsilon \ll 1$, the signal scales in $\epsilon^{-1}$, and therefore increases drastically.
elements.sent-2411-533: This phase shift expression has not been derived in the literature explicitly, in the case of the axion-photon coupling.
elements.sent-2411-534: In Chapter ~\ref{chap:sens_experiments , we will use it to derive the sensitivity of DAMNED to $g_{a\gamma}$.}
elements.sent-2411-535: Phase shift along an optical fiber We now consider a very simple experiment where we use two fibers of same length L, in which we send a left polarized light A in the first one and a right polarized light B in the other one.
elements.sent-2411-536: At the output, we measure the phase shift between both light signals.
elements.sent-2411-537: Mathematically, this situation is equivalent to having only one single fiber and sending both signals in the two opposite directions of the fiber.
elements.sent-2411-538: Both signals have the same angular frequency $\omega_0$ (at zeroth order in the perturbation), and after having traveled through the fiber, the electric fields have the form \begin{subequations \begin{equation}E_A(t) = E^0_A e^{-i\omega_0(t-t_\rightarrow)} \, E_B(t) = E^0_B e^{-i\omega_0(t-t_\leftarrow)}\,\end{equation} \end{subequations with $t_\leftarrow$ and $t_\rightarrow$ being respectively the time for the left and right polarized signals to cross the full fiber.}}
elements.sent-2411-539: At the output of the fiber, the phase shift between the two electric fields is just given by \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\Delta \phi(t) = \omega_0 (t_\leftarrow - t_\rightarrow)\end{equation} where \begin{equation}t_\leftarrow = \frac{L}{c} - \int^{L/c}_0 \frac{\delta c_\leftarrow }{c}\sin(\omega_a t + \Phi) \, t_\rightarrow = \frac{L}{c} - \int^{L/c}_0 \frac{\delta c_\rightarrow }{c}\sin(\omega_a t + \Phi) \, ,\end{equation} \end{subequations where the amplitudes $\delta c_\rightarrow = - \delta c_\leftarrow$ are given in Eq.~\eqref{delta_c_axion_photon_right .}}}
elements.sent-2411-540: Then, it is straightforward to find the amplitude of the phase shift as \begin{equation}\label{eq:phase_fiber_axion_photon} |\Delta \phi(t)| = \frac{2\sqrt{16\pi G \rho_DM} E_P g_{a\gamma}}{\omega_a c} \left|\sin\left(\frac{\omega_a L}{2c}\right)\right| \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-541: Similarly as in the previous section, we will use this result to derive the sensitivity of an optical fiber to $g_{a\gamma}$ in Chapter ~\ref{chap:sens_experiments .}
elements.sent-2411-542: Search for dark photons in a microwave cavity with Rydberg atoms As mentioned in Section ~\ref{DP_photons_coupling , an oscillating DP coupled to the standard electromagnetic field will induce a small oscillating electric field in a vacuum.}
elements.sent-2411-543: One very peculiar feature of this electric field is that it does not propagate, i.e. its wave-vector vanishes $\vec k_U=0$ (to first order in $v_\mathrm{DM /c$).}
elements.sent-2411-544: This is due to the fact that this electric field is induced by a massive vector field and therefore its dispersion relation is given by Eq.~\eqref{dispersion_relation_mass_scalar .}
elements.sent-2411-545: In this chapter, we will show how an electromagnetic cavity can be used to search for the electric field induced by a DP.
elements.sent-2411-546: In addition, we will show how to use atoms as a tool to detect this electric field through the Stark effect, i.e. the displacement of the energy levels of an atom under a perturbation by a static electric field (or by an electric field whose frequency is much lower than the transition frequency of the atom).
elements.sent-2411-547: This experimental proposal to search for DP using atoms inside a microwave cavity is the object of a published article \cite{Gue23 .}
elements.sent-2411-548: There are mainly two reasons to consider a cavity as an experiment to search for DP.
elements.sent-2411-549: First of all, as for other DP experiments using resonators, the mirrors of the cavity will enhance the electric field induced by the DP.
elements.sent-2411-550: Indeed, the electric field parallel to the surface of a perfect conductor has to vanish.
elements.sent-2411-551: Therefore, because of the presence of the oscillating DP-induced electric field, the mirror will generate a standard electromagnetic field that will propagate perpendicularly to the mirror and whose amplitude is such that it will cancel the DP-induced field parallel to the surface.
elements.sent-2411-552: Physically, the DP-induced electric field will induce an oscillation of the electrons within the mirror which will create a standard electromagnetic field.
elements.sent-2411-553: Since a cavity consists in two mirrors, this boundary condition can, under some conditions, produce resonances that will significantly enhance the small DP-induced electric field.
elements.sent-2411-554: The second reason to consider a cavity is related to the use of atoms to measure the electric field inside the cavity through the Stark effect, which is sensitive to the square of the electric field.
elements.sent-2411-555: If one applies a standard electromagnetic wave inside the cavity (whose electric field will be denoted by $\vec E_A$) the DP contribution to the square of the electric field inside the cavity is $\sim \vec E_\mathrm{DM}\cdot \vec E_A$ (to first order in $\chi$, the kinetic mixing between EM and DP), which can also be enhanced by a resonant $\vec E_A$.
elements.sent-2411-556: In addition to enhancing the amplitude of the signal to be measured, applying an external field is also important to produce a signal at low, but non-zero, frequency, where the Stark effect can be realistically measured.
elements.sent-2411-557: More precisely, for a cavity whose length is of the order of a few cm, we will be interested in searching for DP oscillating at a frequency $f_U=\omega_U/2\pi$ of the order of a few GHz.
elements.sent-2411-558: The difficulty is that such a rapid oscillation of the atomic transition frequency will be very hard to measure as the interrogation cycle of the atoms is much longer.
elements.sent-2411-559: But, if one applies en external field at a angular frequency $\omega_A$ which is close to $\omega_U$, then the cross term between the DP electric field and the applied electric field will have a component oscillating at the low angular frequency $\left|\omega_U-\omega_A\right|$, which can be measured by the atoms.
elements.sent-2411-560: As in the previous chapter, we will compute the expected signal, leaving the experimental parameters free.
elements.sent-2411-561: Afterwards, in Chapter ~\ref{chap:exp_summary , we will discuss the sources of noise of such experiment, and in Chapter ~\ref{chap:sens_experiments , we will derive the expected sensitivity of the experiment to the $\chi$ coupling, by considering specific experimental parameters.}}
elements.section-2411-023: Contributions from dark photons and applied electric field inside the cavity
elements.sent-2411-562: In this section, we derive the electric field induced by both the DP and the applied field at the center of the cavity.
elements.sent-2411-563: We summarize the methodology, the derivation and discuss the main results.
elements.sent-2411-564: The finesse $\mathcal{F}$ of a cavity is a measure of the narrowness of resonances $\delta f$ compared to their frequency.
elements.sent-2411-565: More precisely, it is defined as the ratio between $\delta f$ and the frequency separation of resonances $\Delta f$ \begin{equation}F = \frac{\Delta f}{\delta f} \, ,\end{equation} which is frequency independent.
elements.sent-2411-566: Considering a cavity consisting of two flat mirrors of reflectivity $r$ separated by a distance $L$\footnote{For simplicity, we assume that the transverse size of the mirrors is $\gg L,\lambda$, where $\lambda$ is the wavelength of the fields of interest.}
elements.sent-2411-567: While the finesse is usually associated to an optical cavity, it is the quality factor $Q$ that is a more common parameter for microwave cavities.
elements.sent-2411-568: The quality factor is usually defined as the ratio between the energy stored inside the cavity with the energy dissipated.
elements.sent-2411-569: It then becomes obvious that finesse and quality factor are two quantities profoundly connected.
elements.sent-2411-570: The quality factor can also be defined as \cite{Suter14 \begin{equation}Q = \frac{f}{\delta f} \, ,\end{equation} Although being frequency dependent, one can notice that the quality factor of the first resonance of the cavity corresponds exactly to the finesse.}
elements.sent-2411-571: In the following of this chapter, we will assume only the first resonances of our microwave cavity, such that $\mathcal{F \sim Q$.}
elements.sent-2411-572: The principle of calculation is similar for both the DP and the applied electric fields and has already been used in Chapter ~\ref{phase_optical_cavity_axion , inspired from \cite{savallePhD .}}
elements.sent-2411-573: The idea is to propagate the electric field an infinite number of round trips inside the cavity and to sum these infinite number of contributions at a given location.
elements.sent-2411-574: To perform cavity round trips, the field is propagated along one direction and when it reaches a mirror, its amplitude is multiplied by $-r$ and its wave-vector is flipped.
elements.sent-2411-575: For $r<1$, the infinite sum converges and can be calculated explicitly.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{images/DP_cavity_scheme_side.pdf} \caption{Experimental scheme that we propose for the search of the DP-photons coupling : an external field (in blue) is applied at the cavity edge. The standing DP electric field (in yellow) generates a propagating electric field inside the cavity (in orange). At the center of the cavity, the transition frequency of Rydberg atoms is impacted by $|E|^2$ through the Stark effect (see text.).} \label{DP_cavity}
elements.sent-2411-576: First, let us apply this procedure to the applied external field.
elements.sent-2411-577: We assume that the external electric field is applied on the left edge of the cavity, see Fig.~\ref{DP_cavity .}
elements.sent-2411-578: Ideally, the applied field can be parameterized as $\vec E_{A =\vec X_A \Re \left[ e^{-i(\omega_At - k_A \left(x+\frac{L {2}\right)+\phi_A )\right]$, with its amplitude $X_A$, angular frequency $\omega_A$ and phase $\phi_A$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-579: Still assuming a transmission coefficient of the mirror $\sqrt{1-r^2}$, the first contribution at the center of the cavity reads \begin{equation}\vec E^0_A(x=0,t) = \Re \left[\sqrt{1-r^2}\vec{X}_A e^{-i(\omega_At-k_A\frac{L}{2}+\phi_A)}\right]\, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-580: This contribution propagates until the other cavity boundaries, gets reflected once with coefficient $-r$ such that boundary conditions are respected, then comes back to the center, implying that the second contribution reads \begin{equation}\vec E^1_A(x=0,t) = -r\vec E^0_A\left(x=0,t+\frac{L}{c}\right) = \Re\left[-r\sqrt{1-r^2}e^{ik_AL}\vec{X}_A e^{-i(\omega_At-k_A\frac{L}{2}+\phi_A)}\right]\, ,\end{equation} the additional phase $e^{ik_AL}$ shows the time delay of $E^1$ compared to $E^0$ after half a round-trip.
elements.sent-2411-581: This occurs several times and after an infinite number of round trips $N$, the full contribution of the external applied field inside the cavity is \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\vec E^tot_A(x=0,t) = \sum_{n=0}^{N=+\infty} \vec E^{n}_A(x=0,t) \, =\sqrt{1-r^2}\vec X_A\Re \left[e^{-i(\omega_At+\phi_A)}\frac{e^{i\frac{k_AL}{2}}}{1+re^{ik_AL}}\right] \, \equiv \vec A(\omega_A)\cos(\omega_At+\phi_A)+\vec B(\omega_A)\sin(\omega_At+\phi_A) \, ,\label{full_field_applied_simplified}\end{equation} \end{subequations where we assumed $r < 1$ such that $r^N \rightarrow 0$ and with \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\vec A(\omega_A) = \frac{\vec X_A \left(1+r\right)\sqrt{1-r^2}\cos\left(\frac{\omega_AL}{2c}\right)}{1+2r\cos(\frac{\omega_AL}{c})+r^2}\, , \vec B(\omega_A) = \frac{\vec X_A \left(1-r\right)\sqrt{1-r^2}\sin\left(\frac{\omega_AL}{2c}\right)}{1+2r\cos(\frac{\omega_AL}{c})+r^2}\, .\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-582: Let us now focus on the contribution from the DP field.
elements.sent-2411-583: From Eqs.~(\ref{eqs:EDM ), one can, without loss of generality, write the expression of the electric field related to the DP as $\vec E_\mathrm{DM = \vec X_\mathrm{DM \Re \left[ e^{-i\omega_U t}\right]= X_\mathrm{DM \hat e_\mathrm{DM \Re \left[ e^{-i\omega_U t}\right]$ where $\hat e_\mathrm{DM}$ is a unit vector characterizing the polarization of the DP field and $X_\mathrm{DM =\chi c\sqrt{2\mu_0\rho_\mathrm{DM }$ (from Eq.~\eqref{amp_E_field ), and where the phase of the field $\Phi$ disappeared because it can be reabsorbed in $\phi_A$ when computing the interference between the DP and applied fields.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-584: Because of this electric field, both mirrors will generate a propagating standard electromagnetic field such that the total component of the electric field parallel of the mirrors' surface vanishes.
elements.sent-2411-585: The same procedure as above can be realized to know the DM electric field amplitude at the center of the cavity.
elements.sent-2411-586: The subtleties of this calculation are that : 1) the field is emitted by the mirrors towards the center of the cavity, therefore the transmission coefficient $\sqrt{1-r^2}$ factor is not present ; 2) only the DM polarization transverse to the mirror is re-emitted, noted $\vec X_\mathrm{DM,\parallel}$; and 3) there are two different contributions, in phase, each being emitted from one of the edges of the cavity.
elements.sent-2411-587: The total DM contribution at the center is then \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\vec E^tot_DM(x=0,t) = \Re \left[\vec X_DMe^{-i\omega_U t} + 2 \vec{X}_DM,\parallele^{-i(\omega_U t-\frac{kL}{2})}\frac{1}{1+re^{ikL}}\right]\, \label{full_field_DM} \equiv \vec C(\omega_U)\cos(\omega_U t)+\vec D(\omega_U)\sin(\omega_U t) \, , \label{full_field_DM_simplified}\end{equation} \end{subequations with \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\vec C(\omega_U) = \vec X_DM +\frac{2\vec X_DM,\parallel (1+r)\cos(\frac{\omega_U L}{2c})}{1+2r\cos(\frac{\omega_U L}{c})+r^2}\, , \vec D(\omega_U) = \frac{2\vec X_DM,\parallel(1-r)\sin(\frac{\omega_U L}{2c})}{1+2r\cos(\frac{\omega_U L}{c})+r^2}\, .\end{equation}.}}}
elements.formula-2411-151: \begin{equation}\vec E^tot_DM(x=0,t) = \Re \left[\vec X_DMe^{-i\omega_U t} + 2 \vec{X}_DM,\parallele^{-i(\omega_U t-\frac{kL}{2})}\frac{1}{1+re^{ikL}}\right]\, \label{full_field_DM} \equiv \vec C(\omega_U)\cos(\omega_U t)+\vec D(\omega_U)\sin(\omega_U t) \, , \label{full_field_DM_simplified}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-152: \begin{equation}\vec C(\omega_U) = \vec X_DM +\frac{2\vec X_DM,\parallel (1+r)\cos(\frac{\omega_U L}{2c})}{1+2r\cos(\frac{\omega_U L}{c})+r^2}\, , \vec D(\omega_U) = \frac{2\vec X_DM,\parallel(1-r)\sin(\frac{\omega_U L}{2c})}{1+2r\cos(\frac{\omega_U L}{c})+r^2}\, .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-588: The second term is the DM contribution from the cavity, which is almost equivalent, in its form, to the total contribution of the applied field, with an additional factor two, due to the emission of a field from both edges of the cavity (instead of only one for the applied field).
elements.sent-2411-589: The calculations presented in this section have been carried out to leading order in $\mathcal O(\chi)$.
elements.sent-2411-590: In particular, at each interaction between the EM waves and the mirrors a small quantity of EM energy will be transformed into DP.
elements.sent-2411-591: The amplitude of such a process is proportional to $\chi$ and therefore neglected as it contributes terms of order $\mathcal O(\chi^2)$.
elements.sent-2411-592: Furthermore the corresponding energy loss is much smaller than the one coming from the finite reflection coefficient $r$.
elements.sent-2411-593: Total electric field squared As mentioned in the beginning of this chapter, the main idea of the experiment proposed is to detect the hypothetical electric field induced by the DP field by using atoms to measure it through the quadratic Stark effect.
elements.sent-2411-594: The Stark effect consists in a shift in the energy levels of an atom under the perturbation of a static (or slowly evolving\footnote{As long as the angular frequency of oscillation is much smaller than the atomic transition angular frequency from state k to i, $\Delta \omega \ll \omega_{ik}$.}
elements.sent-2411-595: Taking into account both contributions from the applied electric field and the DP field computed previously, the total electric field power at the center of the cavity is \begin{equation}|\vec E(\omega_U,\omega_A)|^2 = \left|\vec E^tot_A +\vec E^tot_DM\right|^2 =\left(\vec A(\omega_A)\cdot \vec C(\omega_U)+\vec B(\omega_A)\cdot \vec D(\omega_U)\right)\cos(\Delta\omega t+\phi_A)+\, \label{general_E_power} \left(\vec B(\omega_A)\cdot \vec C(\omega_U)-\vec A(\omega_A)\cdot \vec D(\omega_U)\right)\sin(\Delta\omega t+\phi_A) + constant \ and \ fast \ oscillating \ terms \, \nonumber ,\end{equation} with $\Delta \omega = \omega_A - \omega_U$.
elements.formula-2411-153: \begin{equation}|\vec E(\omega_U,\omega_A)|^2 = \left|\vec E^tot_A +\vec E^tot_DM\right|^2 =\left(\vec A(\omega_A)\cdot \vec C(\omega_U)+\vec B(\omega_A)\cdot \vec D(\omega_U)\right)\cos(\Delta\omega t+\phi_A)+\, \label{general_E_power} \left(\vec B(\omega_A)\cdot \vec C(\omega_U)-\vec A(\omega_A)\cdot \vec D(\omega_U)\right)\sin(\Delta\omega t+\phi_A) + constant \ and \ fast \ oscillating \ terms \, \nonumber ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-596: In the following, we will not consider the constant terms.
elements.sent-2411-597: Indeed, in the experimental scheme proposed here, we will be interested in the oscillatory behavior of the atomic frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-598: Moreover, we discarded the fast oscillating terms whose angular frequencies are $2\omega_A$, $2\omega_U$ or $\omega_A+\omega_U$, with periods ($\mathcal{O (10^{-9 )$ s) much shorter than the atom interrogation time, such that on average, their impact vanishes.}}
elements.sent-2411-599: From Eq.~\eqref{general_E_power , the signal amplitude can be written as (see Appendix ~\ref{ap:amp_field_cavity for more details) \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\label{eq:Etot2} \frac{\sqrt{1-r^2}X_AX_DM\beta}{\sqrt{1+2r\cos(\frac{\omega_A L}{c})+r^2}}\sqrt{1+4\frac{1+(1+r)\cos(\frac{\omega_U L}{2c})}{1+2r\cos(\frac{\omega_U L}{c})+r^2}} \equiv \chi S(\omega_U,\omega_A;\rho_DM, X_A;L,r)\, ,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\label{beta_DP} \beta = \hat e_DM \cdot \frac{\vec X_A}{X_A} \, ,\end{equation} \end{subequations and $X_\mathrm{DM \propto \chi$, following Eq.~\eqref{amp_E_field .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-600: If the polarization is fixed and does not change each coherence time, $\beta = \cos\theta$, with $\theta$ the angle between $\vec X_A$ and $\vec X_\mathrm{DM}$.
elements.sent-2411-601: If the DP field is isotropically distributed $ \beta=1/\sqrt{3}$.
elements.sent-2411-602: To avoid any orthogonality between the two polarizations, i.e $\beta =0$, one must run the experiment for a significant time, i.e at least several days (see discussion in \cite{Caputo ).}
elements.figure-2411-009: \begin{subfigure}{.5\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth] {images/Signal_Rydberg_3D_1.pdf} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.5\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.65\linewidth]{images/Signal_Rydberg_3D_2.pdf} \end{subfigure} \caption{Signal contribution Eq.~\eqref{eq:Etot2} (arb. units) as function of $\omega_U, \omega_A$, both presented in units of $c/L$. The resonance peaks appear clearly when both frequencies correspond to an odd mode of the cavity.} \label{3D_signal}
elements.sent-2411-603: In Fig.~\ref{3D_signal are shown 3D plots of the signal contribution Eq.~\eqref{eq:Etot2 as function of the angular frequencies of the DM field $\omega_U$ and the applied field $\omega_A$.}}
elements.sent-2411-604: One can notice the various resonance peaks when both frequencies correspond to an odd mode of the cavity.
elements.sent-2411-605: In the experiment scheme presented here, we require the two frequencies to be close, hence we are only interested in regions where $\omega_U \sim \omega_A$.
elements.sent-2411-606: This corresponds to the diagonal of the plot seen from the top.
elements.sent-2411-607: However, in other types of experiment where both frequencies could be separated by some hundreds of MHz or more, other regions of this plot could be studied.
elements.sent-2411-608: The amplitude of the signal is linear in $\chi$ and depends non-linearly on the local DM density, on the amplitude and frequency of the applied external field and on the mass ($m_Uc^2=\hbar \omega_U$) of the DP field.
elements.sent-2411-609: It depends also on some geometric factor of order unity that characterizes how the detector couples to the DP field polarization.
elements.sent-2411-610: For DM and applied frequencies close to odd resonances of the cavity, i.e $\omega_U L/c \approx \omega_A L/c \approx \pi + 2n\pi$, with $n \in \mathbb{N}$, the amplitude Eq.~\eqref{eq:Etot2 becomes proportional to the quality factor $Q$, as expected.}
elements.sent-2411-611: Additionally, as it is also proportional to the injected amplitude $X_A$, we directly see the interesting feature of looking at the field squared amplitude, the applied field acting as an amplification for the DM field.
elements.section-2411-025: Measurement with atoms through the quadratic Stark effect
elements.sent-2411-613: Rydberg atoms The transition frequency measurement can be performed using a regular atomic clock, which allows very good uncertainty on the frequency measurement, but is not very sensitive to the Stark effect e.g. for the $5s^2\,^1S_0 \to 5s5p\,\,^3P_0$ clock transition in Sr the differential polarizability $\Delta \alpha/2h \approx 3.1\times 10^{-6}$~Hz/(V/m)$^2$ \cite{Middelmann , thus requiring a strong applied field.}
elements.sent-2411-614: To overcome this lack of sensitivity to $E^2$, ``regular'' atoms can be replaced by Rydberg atoms, which are in a quantum state with high principal quantum number $n$ \cite{Gallagher_1988 .}
elements.sent-2411-615: The electrons are much further from the nucleus thus the atom has much higher polarizability.
elements.sent-2411-616: For large $n$ the corresponding polarizabilities in Sr can reach $\Delta\alpha/2h \approx 10^{5}$~Hz/(V/m)$^2$ ($n>60$) \cite{Millen2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-617: Typically, $Sr$ Rydberg measurements use laser cooled $Sr$ atoms that are excited to Rydberg states using two photon transitions \cite{Millen2011,Bridge2016 and direct spectroscopy of e.g. the $5s5p\,^3P_1 \to 5sns\,^3S_1$ transition is performed, with $n$ up to 81 \cite{Millen2011,Bridge2016 .}}
elements.sent-2411-618: We give a more explicit summary of the measurement process done in \cite{Bridge2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-619: The atoms are initially in a given atomic state $|i\rangle$ and they transition to a Rydberg state $|r\rangle$ using a UV beam whose frequency (of the order of some hundreds of THz) is continuously controlled to match the transition frequency, that can be perturbed by, e.g., environmental noise.
elements.sent-2411-620: More explicitly, this UV beam is locked on a reference cavity, whose length is modified using a piezo-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-621: This spectroscopic measurement allows one to determine the Rydberg transition frequency with or without the effect of the DP-EM coupling.
elements.sent-2411-622: Now, let us add the effect of such coupling.
elements.sent-2411-623: The quadratic electric field induces a Stark shift on the Rydberg state, implying that at a given time $t$, the transition frequency corresponds to $\nu(t)$ from Eq.~\eqref{signal_stark .}
elements.sent-2411-624: Therefore, in order to maximize the transition probability, the UV light will continuously follow $\nu(t)$, and therefore it will also slowly oscillate at $\Delta \omega$.
elements.sent-2411-625: Afterwards, when the electrons have transitioned to the Rydberg state, another laser pulse is used to ionize them and a small electric field is used to drift the ions to a metallic plate and count them.
elements.sent-2411-626: By counting the ions, one can infer the number of atoms which made the transition to the Rydberg state and therefore measure their transition frequency from the UV beam.
elements.sent-2411-627: Bandwidth of detection In order to see the oscillations at $\Delta\omega$ in the total field power Eq.~\eqref{general_E_power , we require the DM and the applied fields to have different frequencies.}
elements.sent-2411-628: We also require the Nyquist frequency of the apparatus to be higher than the angular frequency of the signal $\Delta \omega < \pi f_s$ to be able to detect any oscillatory behavior in the transition frequency of the atoms.
elements.sent-2411-629: For a given applied angular frequency $\omega_A$, sampling frequency $f_s$, Rydberg atoms perform the measurements of the electric field squared during $T_\mathrm{obs}$ at an angular frequency $\Delta \omega$ (more precisely, $T_\mathrm{obs \times f_s$ measurements will be taken for each $\omega_A$).}
elements.sent-2411-630: The time $T_\mathrm{obs}$ is arbitrary; if it is longer than the measurement process comprising excitation and ionisation, one has to prepare again the atoms to their Rydberg state after de-excitation accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-631: As detailed above, the experiment is sensitive to any $\Delta \omega$ such that $2\pi/T_\mathrm{obs \leq |\Delta \omega| \leq \pi f_s$, which, in terms of DM angular frequency, is equivalent to \begin{equation}\label{eq:DP_Rydberg_bandwidth} \omega_U \in [\omega_A - \pi f_s;\omega_A - \frac{2\pi}{T_obs}] \cup [\omega_A + \frac{2\pi}{T_obs};\omega_A + \pi f_s] \, .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-632: This consists in one measurement sequence which will probe the range of DP masses corresponding to Eq.~\eqref{eq:DP_Rydberg_bandwidth .}
elements.sent-2411-633: The logic of the experiment is then to shift the frequency of the applied electric field to scan another part of the mass range.
elements.sent-2411-634: By applying this procedure for various well chosen $\omega_A$, one can scan a large part of the DP mass range.
elements.sent-2411-635: This scheme can be repeated N times, as much as time allows.
elements.sent-2411-636: At the end of this loop, the total experimental time is simply $T_{\mathrm{tot = NT_\mathrm{obs}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-637: The corresponding total DM frequencies band scanned is $f_{\mathrm{tot = Nf_s = T_{\mathrm{tot f_s/T_\mathrm{obs}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-638: The larger the total experimental time, the larger the band of scanned DM frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-639: Additionally, the blind spots at exact $\omega_A$ can be avoided, and sensitivity can be optimised (see below) by shifting $\omega_A$ by a little less than $2\pi f_s$, at the expense of increasing the overall experimental duration.
elements.sent-2411-640: For DM frequencies in the GHz range, the coherence time of the DM field Eq.~\eqref{coherence_DM $\tau(\omega_U/2\pi) \sim 10^{-3}$ s, i.e it will be considered much smaller than $T_{\mathrm{obs }$, of the order of several seconds.}}
elements.sent-2411-641: Here, we have presented the experimental scheme of an experiment involving Rydberg atoms inside a microwave cavity for the detection of the EM-DP coupling $\chi$.
elements.sent-2411-642: In Chapter ~\ref{chap:exp_summary , we will discuss the sources of noises of the apparatus, which will allow us to derive an expected sensitivity of the experiment in Chapter ~\ref{chap:sens_experiments .. \pagestyle{plain \pagestyle{fancy.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-643: Search for dark photons using dish antennas As a reminder, DP will induce an electric field everywhere in space due to its kinetic mixing with EM.
elements.sent-2411-644: In Chapter ~\ref{chap:Rydberg_exp_DP , we focused on an experiment to enhance this electric field with a cavity.}
elements.sent-2411-645: In this chapter, we will focus on a different way to enhance this electric field, using a spherical dish antenna.
elements.sent-2411-646: Indeed, one can use a dish antenna to focus the small electric field in order to enhance it inside a detector located at the curvature center of the mirror \cite{SHUKET,Tokyo1,Tokyo2,Tokyo3,Tokyo4,Qualiphide, Kotaka23, Knirck23, Bajjali23 .}
elements.sent-2411-647: One of the main assumptions used in existing studies is that the power generated by the dish is entirely focused on the curvature center, hence can be detectable by the detector \cite{Horns .}
elements.sent-2411-648: In this chapter, we will show that this assumption is too optimistic and we will derive an improved modeling for such experiments.
elements.sent-2411-649: More precisely, we will derive analytically the EM field measured by the antenna detector which is highly dependent on the DP Compton frequency.
elements.sent-2411-650: This is done in two steps: (i) we compute the electric field at the location of the antenna and (ii) we determine how much of this electric power is measured by the antenna.
elements.sent-2411-651: This study is the object of an article \cite{Gue:2024gws published in Physical Review D.}
elements.sent-2411-652: For our analysis, we consider a dish of radius $r$ and curvature radius $R$, as depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:SHUKET_Kirchhoff , which works as an EM reflector of the oscillating electric field Eq.~\eqref{eqs:EDM .}}
elements.sent-2411-653: Indeed, boundary conditions $\vec E_\mathrm{tot,\parallel = 0$ at the edge of the dish requires the emission of a regular EM wave, at every point of the dish, directed according to the normal vector of the dish, and with same frequency as the background DM oscillating field, i.e it has the form \begin{equation}\vec E_{D}(\theta,\varphi)=i\chi\omega_U \vec Y_{\parallel,D}(\theta, \varphi) e^{-i\omega_U t} \, , \label{eq:E_out}\end{equation} with $\vec Y_{\parallel,D (\theta,\varphi)$ the component of the DP polarization vector $\vec Y$ defined in Eq.~\eqref{eq:osc_DP , parallel to the radial vector at cylindrical coordinates $(\theta, \varphi)$ such that the electric field parallel to the dish vanishes at the mirror's surface.}}}
elements.sent-2411-654: In addition, we choose a coordinate system whose origin is at the curvature center of the dish.
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[scale=0.4]{images/SHUKET_Kirchhoff_boundaries.pdf} \caption{The oscillating standing DP electric field $\vec E_\mathrm{DM}$ is shown with red arrows. The dish emitter in black, with curvature center shown in purple, acts as reflector and emits a classic propagating electric field $\vec E_\mathrm{D}$ in green. The Kirchhoff integral boundary surfaces $A$ considered for the computation of the electric field Eq.~\eqref{E_Dirichlet} are shown in blue (in addition to the dish, surface $A_1$). It consists of the junction of a semi-sphere centered on the curvature center of the dish with infinite radius (surface $A_3$), and one additional vertical infinite plane surfaces to close the boundary surface (surface $A_2$). This closed surface is chosen such that the contributions from surface $A_2$ and $A_3$ are zero at the curvature center of the dish.} \label{fig:SHUKET_Kirchhoff}
elements.sent-2411-655: In the literature (see e.g \cite{Horns ), one of the main assumptions used in this kind of experiment is that provided that the diameter of the dish $d_\mathrm{dish \gg \lambda$, the wavelength of the emitted field (i.e the Compton wavelength of the DP field), diffraction effects are negligible and the power generated by the dish is entirely focused on the curvature center of the mirror \cite{Horns .}}}
elements.sent-2411-656: If we further assume that the field is homogeneous over the surface of the spherical mirror $A_\mathrm{dish}$ (i.e the DM field coherence length $\ell_\mathrm{DM = \hbar/m_\mathrm{DM v_\mathrm{DM \gg d_\mathrm{dish}$), this total power emitted is \cite{Horns \begin{equation}\label{power_emit_dish} P_emit = A_dish \langle |E_D(\theta,\phi)|^2\rangle = \langle\beta^2\rangle A_dish \chi^2 \rho_DM c\end{equation} where $\beta$ is the projection of the DP field on the surface of the dish, similarly defined as in Eq.~\eqref{beta_DP and $\langle \rangle$ represents the average value over the surface.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-657: Assuming random polarization scenario, $\beta = 2/3$, as we consider a planar emitter.
elements.sent-2411-658: While all previous analysis were based on these assumptions, we will show in this chapter that they are over-optimistic.
elements.sent-2411-659: Indeed, we will provide a more detailed modeling of such experiments by deriving properly the propagation of this emitted field from the dish using Kirchhoff integral.
elements.sent-2411-660: We will show that the amount of power received at the curvature center does depend on a various number of parameters, mainly the Compton frequency of the DP field, and that in some cases, reaches only a few percent of the emitted power, even when the radius of the dish is larger than the wavelength by more than one order of magnitude, i.e when diffraction effects appear negligible.
elements.sent-2411-661: Propagation of the field from the dish to an antenna In this section, we will derive the expression of the total electric field induced by the reflection of the DP field by the dish at a given location.
elements.sent-2411-662: In Sec.~\ref{sec:Kirchhoff , we will give a brief overview of the Kirchhoff method and show that it cannot be directly used to get an analytical expression of the electric field.}
elements.sent-2411-663: Instead, we will decompose the problem into two sub-problems.
elements.sent-2411-664: First, we will propagate the field from the dish to the plane closing the dish (displayed in orange in Fig.~\ref{fig:Dish_plane_Vinet ) using an approximation of the Kirchhoff method valid for thin optical element as presented in Sec.~\ref{sec:dish_plane .}}
elements.sent-2411-665: Subsequently, in Sec.~\ref{sec:plane2receptor , we will propagate the electric field from the plane to the position of the detector using exactly the Kirchhoff method.}
elements.sent-2411-666: The Kirchhoff theorem allows one to solve the wave equation at a given position $\vec x$ by computing an integral over a closed surface around $\vec x$ \cite{Jackson .}
elements.sent-2411-667: In practice, this means that one can derive the electric field at position $\vec x$ as function of the field at all points on this closed surface.
elements.sent-2411-668: In this formalism, the temporal dependence of the field is separated from its spatial dependence.
elements.sent-2411-669: Therefore, we decompose the emitted electric field at the dish's surface as \begin{equation}\vec E_D(\vec x',t) = \Re[\vec U_D(\vec x')e^{-i\omega t}]\, , \label{eq:Uout}\end{equation} where the complex function $\vec U_D(\vec x')$ denotes the spatial part of the field and $\vec x'$ is a point on the dish's surface.
elements.sent-2411-670: We now consider a closed surface, denoted A, which encloses the point $\vec x$ where the value of the field is calculated.
elements.sent-2411-671: From Kirchhoff integral theorem, the general expression of $\vec U_D$ is \cite{Jackson , \begin{equation}\label{vector_Kirchhoff} \vec{U}_D(\vec{x}) = \int_{A} dS' \left(\vec{U}_D(\vec{x'})(\hat{n}'\cdot \vec \nabla G(\vec{x},\vec{x}')) - G(\vec{x},\vec{x}')(\hat{n}' \cdot \vec \nabla) \vec{U}_D(\vec{x'})\right)\, ,\end{equation} where $\hat n'$ is a unit vector normal to the surface A directed inwardly, the derivatives are with respect to the emission coordinates $x'$ and $G$ is a Green function, appropriately defined for the situation.}
elements.sent-2411-672: In the situation depicted in the previous section, from Eq.~\eqref{eq:E_out , we know exactly the components of the electric field at the surface of the emitting dish.}
elements.sent-2411-673: Therefore, an appropriate Green function is the Dirichlet Green function $G_D$ \cite{Jackson \begin{equation}G_D(\vec{x},\vec{x}')= 0 \quad \forall \: \vec x' \in A \, .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-674: Then, Eq.~\eqref{vector_Kirchhoff becomes \begin{equation}\label{E_Dirichlet} \vec U_D(\vec{x}) = \int_{A} \mathrm{dS} \left(\vec U_D(\vec{x'}) (\vec \nabla G_D(\vec{x},\vec{x}')\cdot \hat{n}')\right)\, .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-675: In the following of the chapter, unless otherwise specified, whenever we mention electric fields, we refer to $\vec U_D$, i.e its spatial part, following Eq.~\eqref{E_Dirichlet .}
elements.sent-2411-676: In the present situation, the closed surface A is appropriately defined by the junction of the dish, a surface with radius $R\rightarrow \infty$ and one additional plane surface along $(x,y)$ plane to close the surface, such that only the field emitted by the dish will contribute.
elements.sent-2411-677: The Kirchhoff integral surface boundary is shown in blue in Fig.~\ref{fig:SHUKET_Kirchhoff .}
elements.sent-2411-678: Our system is therefore composed of a spherical dish and Eq.~\eqref{E_Dirichlet requires the derivation of a Dirichlet Green function for a portion of sphere.}
elements.sent-2411-679: However, no analytical expression has been found for such geometry, which makes it impossible to find an exact analytical solution for our problem.
elements.sent-2411-680: Instead, we will decompose the problem into two sub-problems: using an approximation, we will propagate the electric field from the dish to the plane closing the dish (see Fig.~\ref{fig:Dish_plane_Vinet ) and then, using the Kirchhoff integral, from the plane up to the detector.}
elements.sent-2411-681: Propagation of the electric field from the dish to the plane This situation is depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:Dish_plane_Vinet , where the distance between the fictional plane (orange on the figure) and the dish at coordinates $x=y=0$ is noted $a$.}
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[scale=0.22]{images/SHUKET_dish_plane_Pierre.pdf} \caption{To compute the electric field induced by the dish at a location $P$ at cylindrical coordinates $(\rho,\phi,z)$, we first propagate the electric field emitted by the spherical dish (in light grey) into the fictional plane (in orange) following Eq.~\eqref{Vinet}. Then, we propagate this electric field to the receiver using the Kirchhoff integral. This procedure is valid only for a spherical dish with low curvature, i.e whose radius $r$ is much smaller than its curvature radius $R$.} \label{fig:Dish_plane_Vinet}
elements.sent-2411-682: As mentioned above, it is not possible to find an analytical solution to Eq.~(\ref{E_Dirichlet ) for this particular geometry.}
elements.sent-2411-683: Instead, we use the thin optical element approximation presented in details in Sec.
elements.sent-2411-684: This approximation is valid if the two following conditions are fulfilled:.
elements.sent-2411-685: The transverse propagation modes $p,q$ (which can be viewed as the coordinates of the wavevector in the $x-y$ plane \cite{Vinet ) need to be much smaller than the longitudinal one $k$.}
elements.sent-2411-686: In our case, the transverse modes are determined by the spatial extent of the dish antenna in the $x-y$ plane $\Delta x, \Delta y$, with $p \sim q \sim 1/\Delta x \sim 1 \: \mathrm{m ^{-1 \ll \omega_U/c = k \sim 10^3 \: \mathrm{m ^{-1}$, in the GHz range.}}}
elements.sent-2411-687: In addition, the galactic velocity distribution in the DM halo induces a transverse contribution \cite{An23 , of the order of $\omega_U v_\mathrm{DM /c^2 \sim 3 \times 10^{-3 \: \mathrm{m ^{-1 \ll \omega_U/c\sim k$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-689: The dish needs to have a low curvature, or in other words, the radius is much smaller than the curvature radius $r \ll R$.
elements.sent-2411-690: Therefore, in the following, we will restrict ourselves to low curvature dish emitter.
elements.sent-2411-691: A straightforward but lengthy calculation presented in details in \cite{Vinet shows that, under the conditions detailed above, the Kirchhoff integral Eq.~\eqref{E_Dirichlet simply reduces to \begin{equation}\vec U_P\left(x,y,z_plane\right) = -e^{ikf(x,y)}\vec U_D\left(x,y,z_dish\left(x,y\right)\right)\, , \label{Vinet}\end{equation} where $\vec U_D$ is the electric field emitted by the dish and $\vec U_P$ is the electric field at the location of the closing plane.}}
elements.sent-2411-692: Furthermore, the function $f(x,y)=z_\mathrm{dish}\left(x,y\right)-z_\mathrm{plane}$ is the surface equation of the dish.
elements.sent-2411-693: In other words, within the approximation of thin optical element, the curvature of the dish translates into simple phase factors determined by the distance between the closing plane and the dish \cite{Vinet .}
elements.sent-2411-694: We now introduce a cylindrical coordinate system ($\rho,\phi,z$), see Fig.~\ref{fig:Dish_plane_Vinet .}
elements.sent-2411-695: Because of the cylindrical symmetry, the surface equation that writes \begin{equation}f(x,y) =f(\rho) = \sqrt{R^2-\rho^2}-R+a \approx \frac{r^2 -\rho^2}{2R} \, , \label{eq:f}\end{equation} depends only on the cylindrical coordinate $\rho$, where we used $a \approx r^2/2R$, since the dish is a portion of sphere, with small curvature.
elements.sent-2411-696: Inserting the spatial part of the field on the dish from Eq.~\eqref{eq:E_out into Eq.~(\ref{Vinet ), we can now estimate the expression of the field in this fictional plane as \begin{equation}\label{eq:E_plane} \vec U_P(\rho,\phi,z_\mathrm{plane})=-i\chi \omega_U e^{ikf(\rho)}\vec Y_{\parallel, D}(\rho,\phi,z_\mathrm{plane}+f(\rho))\, ,\end{equation} where $\rho\leq r$ and $z_\mathrm{plane =R-a$ is the $z$-coordinate of the plane.}}}
elements.sent-2411-697: This expression is only valid in the thin optical element approximation, whose conditions are detailed at the beginning of this section.
elements.sent-2411-698: Propagation of the electric field from the plane to the receiver We can now focus on the propagation from an emitting plane, a subject that has been vastly treated in the literature, e.g in \cite{Jackson, Vinet .}
elements.sent-2411-699: The idea is to use the Kirchhoff integral from Eq.~(\ref{vector_Kirchhoff ) using an appropriate Dirichlet Green function that vanishes on the plane.}
elements.sent-2411-700: In the case of a plane emitter located at $z_\mathrm{plane =R-a$, such a Dirichlet Green function is given by \begin{equation}G_D(\vec x,\vec x')=\frac{e^{ik L'}}{4\pi L'} -\frac{e^{ik L''}}{4\pi L''}\, , \label{Dirichlet}\end{equation} where \cite{Jackson,Vinet \begin{subequations \begin{equation}L' = |\vec x - \vec x'| = \sqrt{\rho^2+\rho'^2-2\rho\rho'\cos(\phi-\phi')+(R-a+\Delta z-z')^2}\, L'' = |\vec x - \vec x''| = \sqrt{\rho^2+\rho'^2-2\rho\rho'\cos(\phi-\phi')+(R-a-\Delta z-z')^2}\, ,\end{equation} \end{subequations with $\vec x''$ the point symmetrical to $\vec x'$ with respect to the plane.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-168: \begin{equation}G_D(\vec x,\vec x')=\frac{e^{ik L'}}{4\pi L'} -\frac{e^{ik L''}}{4\pi L''}\, , \label{Dirichlet}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-169: \begin{equation}L' = |\vec x - \vec x'| = \sqrt{\rho^2+\rho'^2-2\rho\rho'\cos(\phi-\phi')+(R-a+\Delta z-z')^2}\, L'' = |\vec x - \vec x''| = \sqrt{\rho^2+\rho'^2-2\rho\rho'\cos(\phi-\phi')+(R-a-\Delta z-z')^2}\, ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-701: In Eq.~\eqref{eq:Green_distances , we defined \begin{equation}\Delta z = z- (R-a) \, ,\end{equation} the difference of $z$ coordinates between the receiving point $z < z'$ and the emitter on the plane.}
elements.sent-2411-702: One can easily show that this property leads to $L'=L''$ if $x'$ is on the plane and therefore the required condition $G_D(\vec x, \vec x_\mathrm{plane ) = 0$ is satisfied.}
elements.sent-2411-703: To compute Eq.~\eqref{E_Dirichlet , we use the directional derivative since $\hat n' = \hat z'$ \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\vec \nabla G_D(\vec{x},\vec{x}')\cdot \hat{n}' = \frac{\partial G_D}{\partial z'}\, =-\frac{(ik L'-1)(R-a+\Delta z-z')e^{ik L'}}{4\pi L'^3}+\frac{(ik L''-1)(R-a-\Delta z-z')e^{ik L''}}{4\pi L''^3}\, .\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-171: \begin{equation}\vec \nabla G_D(\vec{x},\vec{x}')\cdot \hat{n}' = \frac{\partial G_D}{\partial z'}\, =-\frac{(ik L'-1)(R-a+\Delta z-z')e^{ik L'}}{4\pi L'^3}+\frac{(ik L''-1)(R-a-\Delta z-z')e^{ik L''}}{4\pi L''^3}\, .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-704: Then, the electric field on the fictional plane propagating towards $z<R-a$ reads \begin{equation}\vec U_dish(\rho,\phi, z) \approx -\frac{i\chi \omega_U \Delta z}{2\pi}\int_0^r d\rho' \rho'e^{ikf(\rho')} \int_0^{2\pi}d\phi' \frac{ikL-1}{L^3}e^{ikL}\vec Y_{\parallel, D}(\rho',\phi',f(\rho')+R-a) \, , \label{E_field_complete}\end{equation} This result provides the expression of the electric field induced by the DP field reflected by the dish anywhere in space under the approximation that the dish is a thin optical element.
elements.formula-2411-172: \begin{equation}\vec U_dish(\rho,\phi, z) \approx -\frac{i\chi \omega_U \Delta z}{2\pi}\int_0^r d\rho' \rho'e^{ikf(\rho')} \int_0^{2\pi}d\phi' \frac{ikL-1}{L^3}e^{ikL}\vec Y_{\parallel, D}(\rho',\phi',f(\rho')+R-a) \, , \label{E_field_complete}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-705: In particular, this formula includes diffraction effects that were implicitly neglected in previous studies \cite{Horns .}
elements.sent-2411-706: This integral is generally not solvable analytically and some approximations might be made to simplify it.
elements.sent-2411-707: Detection of the electric field with a horn antenna While the previous section was devoted to the emission and propagation of the electric field induced by the dark photon and enhanced by the dish, we will now focus on the detection of this electric field.
elements.sent-2411-708: In this section, we consider the detection system to be a horn antenna of long side $A$ and small side $B$.
elements.sent-2411-709: At the output of the antenna is located a resistance $R_0$ such that when an oscillating electric field is applied to the horn antenna, it is translated into a measurable voltage.
elements.sent-2411-710: We will compare two different approaches to estimate this measurement: the first one is based on an analytical calculation which computes the overlap integral between the electric field at the location of the antenna and the antenna mode ; the second one consists in using the antenna gain factor provided by the antenna's manufacturer.
elements.sent-2411-711: Let us first consider an antenna of internal resistance $R_0$ as an emitter by applying an oscillating voltage $V(t) = V_0 \cos (\omega_U t)$ to its terminals.
elements.sent-2411-712: The reciprocity theorem ensures that this will be equivalent to considering the antenna as a receiver.
elements.sent-2411-713: As a result, the antenna will emit an electric field predominantly in the TE$_{10}$ mode, i.e. polarized parallel to the small side of the rectangular horn, which is the most widely used fundamental mode for pyramidal horn antennas \cite{Balanis05 .. \newline.}
elements.figure-2411-012: \begin{minipage}{\textwidth} \begin{minipage}{0.42\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{images/antenne_v2.pdf} \caption{Rectangular horn antenna and definition of its physical surface aperture.} \label{fig:antenna} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.55\textwidth} Therefore, in a coordinate system with the $z$-axis perpendicular to the surface aperture of the antenna, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:antenna}, the electric field generated by the antenna can be written as \begin{equation}\label{eq:E_mode_ant} \vec E_ant(t,x,y,z) = \Re\left[V_ant \vec M_ant(x,y) e^{i(kz-\omega_U t+\phi)}\right] \, ,\end{equation} where $\phi$ is the phase of the electric field and $V_\mathrm{ant}$ characterizes the amplitude of this field (Energy conservation implies that $V_0^2/2R_0=V_\mathrm{ant}^2/2Z_0$, where $Z_0=376.7 \: \Omega$ is the vacuum impedance.). \newline \end{minipage} \end{minipage}
elements.sent-2411-714: The real antenna mode can be written as \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\label{eq:mode_ant} \vec M_ant(x,y) = m_{TE_{10}}\hat{y}\cos\left( \frac{\pi x}{A_eff} \right) \,,\end{equation} where $A_\mathrm{eff}$ is the effective width and $m_{TE_{10 }$ (dimension L$^{-1}$) is a normalisation factor that ensures the mode is normalized $\int dS_\mathrm{eff |\vec M_\mathrm{ant (x,y)|^2 = 1$ on the effective aperture of the antenna, i.e. \begin{equation}\label{eq:m_value} m_{TE_{10}} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{S_eff}} \,.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-715: Indeed, the effective area of the horn, which can be expressed as the product of an effective width $A_\mathrm{eff}$ with an effective height $B_\mathrm{eff}$, depends on the frequency as \cite{Balanis05 \begin{equation}S_eff(\omega_U) = A_eff(\omega_U)B_eff(\omega_U)=\frac{e_r\pi G(\omega_U)c^2}{\omega^2_U} \, , \label{eq:effective_surface}\end{equation} where $e_r$ is the realistic efficiency of the antenna, which represents reflection losses inside the antenna, and where $G(\omega)$ is the frequency dependent gain of the antenna \cite{Balanis05 .}}
elements.sent-2411-716: Note that according to the IEEE Standards, the gain does not include reflection and polarization losses \cite{Balanis05 , this is why we separate $e_r$ from $G(\omega)$.}
elements.sent-2411-717: The time-averaged electromagnetic power generated by the antenna is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:Pout} P_\mathrm{out} = \frac{1}{2Z_0} \int dS_\mathrm{eff} \left|\vec E_\mathrm{ant}\right|^2 =\frac{V_\mathrm{ant}^2}{2Z_0} \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-718: We will now invert this reasoning and consider the antenna as a receiver that will output a voltage in response to an electric field \begin{equation}\label{eq:E_def} \vec E(t,x,y,z)=\Re[\vec U(x,y,z)e^{-i\omega_U t}]= \left|\vec U\right|\hat e_U\cos(\omega_U t +\varphi) \, ,\end{equation} where $\varphi$ is an irrelevant phase, $\hat e_U$ is the unit polarization vector of $\vec U$, $\left|\vec U\right|=\left(\vec U\cdot \vec U^\dagger \right)^{1/2}$ denotes the complex modulus.
elements.formula-2411-177: \begin{equation}\label{eq:E_def} \vec E(t,x,y,z)=\Re[\vec U(x,y,z)e^{-i\omega_U t}]= \left|\vec U\right|\hat e_U\cos(\omega_U t +\varphi) \, ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-719: The antenna will measure electric fields that are propagating into the $\hat z$ direction and matching its mode $\vec M_\mathrm{ant (x,y)$.}
elements.sent-2411-720: More precisely, by taking the dot product of Eq.~(\ref{eq:E_mode_ant ) with $\vec M_\mathrm{ant}$, one gets $V_\mathrm{ant}\cos \left(\omega t -kz -\phi\right)=\int dS_\mathrm{eff \vec M_\mathrm{ant (x,y)\cdot \vec E_\mathrm{ant (t,x,y,z)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-721: Using the reciprocity properties of electromagnetism, one can replace $\vec E_\mathrm{ant}$ by the electric field we are trying to measure Eq.~(\ref{eq:E_def ).}
elements.sent-2411-722: This reasoning shows that the antenna will output a voltage proportional to \begin{equation}\label{eq:antenna_output_gen} V_ant(z)\cos(\omega_U t+\phi'(z))=\cos(\omega_U t+\varphi) \int dS_eff(\omega_U) \left|\vec U(x,y,z)\right| \hat e_U \cdot \vec M_ant(x,y) \, ,\end{equation} where $\phi'(z)=-kz-\phi$ and the 2D integral is carried out in the $x-y$ plane at the $z$-coordinate of the antenna.
elements.formula-2411-178: \begin{equation}\label{eq:antenna_output_gen} V_ant(z)\cos(\omega_U t+\phi'(z))=\cos(\omega_U t+\varphi) \int dS_eff(\omega_U) \left|\vec U(x,y,z)\right| \hat e_U \cdot \vec M_ant(x,y) \, ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-723: Let us factorize the electric field generated by the dish at the location of the antenna provided by Eq.~(\ref{E_field_complete ) in a voltage factor $V_\mathrm{dish}$, a real mode factor $\vec M_\mathrm{dish}$ and a plane wave factor, such that it takes the following form in the antenna aperture plane $(x,y)$ \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\label{eq:E_mode_dish} \vec {E}_dish(t,x,y,z) = V_dish \vec{M}_dish(x,y,z) \cos(\omega t+\phi'(z)) \equiv \left|\vec U_dish(x,y,z)\right|\hat e_U\cos(\omega t+\varphi) \, ,\end{equation} where $\vec{M _\mathrm{dish (x,y,z)$ is normalized over the infinite surface of the antenna plane $S_\infty$ at $z=z_\mathrm{ant}$ i.e \begin{equation}\int dS_\infty |\vec{M}_dish(x,y,z_ant)|^2 = 1 \, . \label{mode_dish_norm}\end{equation} \end{subequations The constant amplitude $V_\mathrm{dish}$ in Eq.~\eqref{eq:E_mode_dish can be computed through energy conservation: the total energy generated by the dish should equal the energy received on the infinite antenna plane $S_\infty $.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-724: Then, using Eqs.~\eqref{power_emit_dish and \eqref{mode_dish_norm , we have \begin{equation}P_dish = \int dS_\infty \frac{\left|\vec U_dish\right|^2}{2Z_0} =\frac{V_dish^2}{2Z_0} \Rightarrow V_dish = \sqrt{\frac{4Z_0A_dish\rho_DMc}{3}}\chi \label{eq:Vdish} \, .\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-725: Then, in our experimental scheme, the time averaged power generated by the horn antenna is obtained by combining Eqs.~\eqref{eq:Pout , ~\eqref{eq:antenna_output_gen and \eqref{eq:E_mode_dish and is provided by \begin{equation}\label{eq:overlap_int} P_\mathrm{rec}(z_\mathrm{ant},\omega_U) = \frac{V_\mathrm{dish}^2}{2Z_0}\left(\int dS_\mathrm{eff}(\omega_U) \vec{M}_\mathrm{dish}(x,y,z_\mathrm{ant})\cdot \vec{M}_\mathrm{ant}(x,y)\,\right)^2 \, ,\end{equation} where $\vec M_\mathrm{dish}$ depends explicitly on $z_\mathrm{ant}$ and where the integral is performed at every position $(x,y)$ on the effective surface of the horn antenna.}}}
elements.sent-2411-726: Then, the ratio between the time averaged power measured by the horn antenna and the total power emitted by the dish is simply given by \begin{equation}\gamma(z_ant,\omega_U)_Overlap = \left(\int dS_eff(\omega_U) \vec{M}_dish(x,y,z_ant)\cdot \vec{M}_ant(x,y)\right)^2 \, , \label{ratio_powers}\end{equation} where $\vec{M _\mathrm{dish}$ directly depends on the Kirchhoff integral Eq.~\eqref{E_field_complete .}}
elements.sent-2411-727: Another approach to estimate the output of the horn antenna is to consider the antenna factor (AF), a characteristic of the antenna provided by the manufacturer.
elements.sent-2411-728: The AF is defined by considering an incident plane wave, or in other words, an incoming electromagnetic field whose mode is constant over the aperture of the antenna.
elements.sent-2411-729: It is defined by (see e.g. \cite{Mclean02 ) \begin{equation}AF(\omega_U) = \frac{\left|\vec U_dish(\omega_U)\right|}{V_0} = \frac{V_dish M_dish}{V_0} \, ,\end{equation} where $V_\mathrm{0}$ is the generated tension, $\vec U_\mathrm{dish}$ the amplitude of the incoming electric field, $\vec M_\mathrm{dish = M_\mathrm{dish \hat e_U$ from Eq.~\eqref{eq:E_mode_dish , with ${M _\mathrm{dish}$ the value of the constant mode of the electric field on the effective antenna area.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-730: The AF depends on the frequency of the incident field.
elements.sent-2411-731: It is measured experimentally and therefore takes into account any loss inside the antenna, represented by the $e_r$ parameter.

elements.part-2411-005: Search through tests of the equivalence principle
elements.sent-2411-742: Classical tests of the equivalence principle.
elements.sent-2411-743: Experiments testing the equivalence principle As described in Section ~\ref{EP_general , EP is not a fundamental symmetry of the universe, and many Beyond the Standard Model (BSM) theories predict its violation \cite{Uzan11, Damour12,Damour94,hees18,Bertone18,Hui17 , in particular by the spacetime variation of fundamental constants such as the fine structure constant $\alpha$ or the electron mass $m_e$.}}
elements.sent-2411-744: In order to quantify such violation, one can measure the acceleration of two test bodies A and B in an external gravitational field and compare them through the so-called E\"otv\"os parameter defined as \begin{equation}\label{Eotvos_param} \eta = 2\frac{(\vec a_A - \vec a_B)}{|\vec a_A + \vec a_B|}\cdot \hat e_meas\end{equation} where $\hat e_\mathrm{meas}$ is the sensitive axis of the apparatus considered.
elements.sent-2411-745: The MICROSCOPE mission consists in a micro-satellite launched in circular orbit around Earth at a 710 km altitude \cite{Microscope17 .}
elements.sent-2411-746: It is the first mission aiming at measuring a violation of EP in space, taking advantage of a very quiet environment and long durations of free fall.
elements.sent-2411-747: It was launched on 25 april 2016 for a total duration of 120 orbits of the satellite around Earth \cite{Berge23 , which as we shall see in Chapter ~\ref{chap:sens_experiments corresponds approximately to $8.1 \times 10^6$ s integration time.}}
elements.sent-2411-748: The satellite measured the difference of acceleration between a Platinum and a Titanium based test masses and it is sensitive to both Eqs.~\eqref{EP_viol_acc_dil and \eqref{EP_viol_acc_axion .}}
elements.sent-2411-749: Their final constraint on $\eta$ is \cite{Microscope22 \begin{equation}\label{eq:eta_MICROSCOPE} \eta= (-1.5 \pm 2.8)\times 10^{-15}\, ,\end{equation} at a 1$\sigma$ confidence-level.}
elements.sent-2411-750: Torsion balance is an apparatus able to measure very weak forces, once used by Cavendish to measure the gravitational constant $G$, and now used for the measurement of the weak EP.
elements.sent-2411-751: In usual torsion balances experiments, a bar is suspended by a thin fiber, which acts as a torsion spring.
elements.sent-2411-752: If a force is applied to one of the ends of the bar with direction perpendicular to it, the bar rotates and twists the fiber.
elements.sent-2411-753: This way, the magnitude of this unknown force can be derived by measuring the rotation angle of the bar.
elements.sent-2411-754: By setting two different test masses with two different compositions at each end of the bar (but with the same mass), one can measure the differential gravitational acceleration that act on them and therefore a violation of the equivalence principle.
elements.sent-2411-755: The current best constraint on the E\"otv\"os parameter from Torsion balances experiments is \cite{Schlamminger08 \begin{equation}\eta = (0.3 \pm 1.8) \times 10^{-13} \, ,\end{equation} at a 1$\sigma$ confidence-level.}
elements.sent-2411-756: Thanks to retro-reflectors array installation on the surface of the Moon by several american and russian space missions, starting from 1969, it is now possible to measure the Earth-Moon distance by sending light signals to this array and measure the round trip time of photons.
elements.sent-2411-757: This is what is called Lunar Laser Ranging (LLR).
elements.sent-2411-758: Both the Earth and the Moon have different compositions and are in the solar gravitational potential, a violation of the EP would imply the two systems fall at different rates towards the Sun.
elements.sent-2411-759: This would distort the lunar orbit, which could be measuring using LLR \cite{Williams09 .}
elements.sent-2411-760: Since it would be unsatisfactory to model planetary and stellar bodies as point-like particles, we must include the self gravitation energy of each body, which is covered by the strong EP.
elements.sent-2411-761: In other words, LLR measurements provide us a constrain on a combination of strong and weak EP, but not on one of them individually.
elements.section-2411-030: Acceleration difference induced by oscillating rest mass
elements.sent-2411-762: As derived in Sections ~\ref{dilaton_pheno and \ref{axion_gluon_pheno , axion and dilaton fields can produce oscillations of the rest mass and transition frequencies of atoms from their different couplings with SM fields.}}

elements.sent-2411-764: The rest mass and transition frequency oscillations of an atom A are shown in Eq.~\eqref{mass_freq_osc_dilaton , which leads to an UFF violating acceleration Eq.~\eqref{EP_viol_acc_dil \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\vec a_A(t, \vec x) = \left[\omega_\phi \vec v_A-\vec k_\phi c^2\right]\frac{\sqrt{16 \pi G \rho_DM}[Q^A_M]_d}{\omega_\phi c}\sin(\omega_\phi t - \vec k_\phi \cdot \vec x +\Phi) \, \approx \frac{\sqrt{16 \pi G \rho_DM}\vec v_DM[Q^A_M]_d}{c}\sin(\omega_\phi t - \vec k_\phi \cdot \vec x +\Phi) \label{eq:a_UFF_lab_frame}\end{equation} \end{subequations where, as explained below Eq.~\eqref{EP_viol_acc_dil , we immediately got rid off the transition frequency contribution to the acceleration, as it is suppressed by a factor $\hbar \omega^0_A/m^0_A c^2 \sim 10^{-10}$.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-765: At the second line, we used the fact that $-\vec k_\phi c^2 = \omega_\phi \vec v_\mathrm{DM}$ and $v_\mathrm{DM \gg v_A$.}
