elements.figure-2411-020: \includegraphics[width=0.6\textwidth]{images/LISA_TF.pdf} \caption{Amplitude of transfer functions of TDI $X$ combinations for both scalar ULDM (in blue) and GW (in orange) from Eqs.~\eqref{eq:amp_TF_X2} and \eqref{eq:TDI2_GW_TF} respectively. For this plot, we neglected the impact of the geometric factors.} \label{fig:TF_LISA}
elements.sent-2411-1075: As it can be noticed from Eqs.~\eqref{eq:TDI_X2_DM_low_freq and \eqref{eq:TDI_X2_GW_low_freq and in Fig.~\ref{fig:TF_LISA , at low frequency, the DM and GW transfer function do not scale equally as function of the frequency, in particular it exists a third order term in $\omega L/c$ in the GW transfer function and not in the DM one.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1076: There are two reasons for this difference.
elements.sent-2411-1077: The first one is that the GW travels at speed $c$, while the DM field is non relativistic.
elements.sent-2411-1078: Indeed, one can notice that assuming a "relativistic DM" which travels at speed $c$ instead of $10^{-3}\:c$, the transfer function Eq.~\eqref{eq:TF_X2_DM_full scales as $(\omega L/c)^3$ at low frequency, as the GW transfer function.}
elements.sent-2411-1079: This suggests that for a dipolar signal, the velocity of the wave impacts the scaling of the transfer function on frequency.
elements.sent-2411-1080: The second reason is that DM is dipolar while GW is quadrupolar.
elements.sent-2411-1081: Indeed, assuming now a non-relativistic GW (i.e neglecting terms $\propto kL \ll 1$), one can notice that the GW transfer function Eq.~\eqref{eq:TDI2_GW_TF also scales as $(\omega L/c)^3$ at low frequency, as the relativistic GW.}
elements.sent-2411-1082: This suggests that for a quadrupolar signal, the velocity of the wave does not influence the scaling of the transfer function on the frequency.
elements.sent-2411-1083: Therefore, it is the fact that the DM produces a non-relativistic and dipolar signal which makes a difference in transfer function compared to the GW.
elements.sent-2411-1084: Now, let us give a physical explanation.
elements.sent-2411-1085: Assuming a low frequency and non-relativistic wave (i.e $\omega \ll c/L \equiv 1/\tau$, where $\tau$ is the light travel time between spacecrafts ; and with velocity $v \ll c$, such that the wavelength is much larger than the armlength $\lambda \gg L$), the phase of the wave at the location of the emitting and receiving spacecrafts is very close (i.e $\Delta \Phi \ll 2\pi$) and therefore, the one-way Doppler seen by the photon is small as well (from Eq.~\eqref{eq:TF_DM , it is $\mathcal{O (\omega L/c)$).}}
elements.sent-2411-1086: If we now assume a full round trip emitter-receiver-emitter, the wave has not propagated much and therefore, the outward effect is, at leading order, the same as the inward effect (up to a sign).
elements.sent-2411-1087: Therefore, for DM, since we are essentially looking at this inward-outward effect projected on a dipole, the signal decreases (for example, $\mathcal{T ^\mathrm{DM _{13 +\exp(-i\omega L/c)\mathcal{T ^\mathrm{DM _{31}$ is $\mathcal{O (\omega L/c)^2$).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1088: For the quadrupolar GW, the outward effect is still almost the same as the inward effect but they also share the same sign, therefore the signal is still $\mathcal{O (\omega L/c)$.}
elements.sent-2411-1089: This is why the polarization makes a difference (for non relativistic waves).
elements.sent-2411-1090: Now, if we assume that both waves are relativistic, but the polarization still differs, the phase of oscillation between the emitting and receiving spacecrafts will be different for both dipolar and quadrupolar waves (even for low frequencies), and therefore, both effects are of the same order of magnitude.
elements.sent-2411-1091: This is why DM, as a non relativistic wave and with a dipolar effect on LISA arm, has not the same transfer function as GW.
elements.subsection-2411-041: Summary of the expected one-arm signals
elements.sent-2411-1092: In summary, if we consider an ULDM or GW background that produces a Doppler shift at angular frequency $\omega$ (i.e $\omega = \omega_\phi = 2 \omega_a = \omega_U = \omega_\mathrm{GW}$), one can write the one-link Doppler shift in a very concise way, similarly as in \cite{Yu23 \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\label{eq:Doppler_LISA} y_{re} = \Re\left[\mu_{re} \left(e^{i(\omega t - \vec k \cdot \vec x_r + \Phi)}-e^{i(\omega t - \frac{\omega L}{c} - \vec k \cdot \vec x_e + \Phi)}\right)\right] \, ,\end{equation} where the wavevector $\vec k$ depends on the velocity of the wave i.e $|\vec k|=\omega/c$ for GW and $|\vec k|=\omega v_\mathrm{DM /c^2$ for DM, and with the unitless $\mu_\mathrm{re}$ are given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:geo_factor_DM_GW} \mu_{re} =  \displaystyle \left(\hat n_{re} \cdot \hat e_v\right)\frac{\sqrt{16 \pi G \rho_DM}v_DM[Q_M]_d}{\omega c^2} \: \: \: \: for \:DM \: pure \: scalar \: field \, \displaystyle \left(\hat n_{re} \cdot \hat e_v \right)\frac{32 \pi G \rho_DM v_DM E^2_P[Q_M]_a}{f^2_a \omega c^3} \: \: \: \: for \: DM \: pseudo \: scalar \: field \, \displaystyle -\left(\hat n_{re} \cdot \hat e_Y \right)\frac{\sqrt{2\mu_0 \rho_DM}\epsilon e [Q_B-L]}{\omega m_TM} \: \: \: \: for \: DM \: vector \: field \, \displaystyle -\left(\frac{\hat n^i_{re}\hat n^j_{re}\hat h^SSB_{ij}(\imath, \Psi)}{2(1-\hat n_{re} \cdot \hat k)}\right) A \: \: \: \: for \: gravitational \: wave \end{equation} \end{subequations and where we neglected the frequency derivative in the case of the GW $\dot f = 0$, for simplicity and concise equations purposes.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-254: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Doppler_LISA} y_{re} = \Re\left[\mu_{re} \left(e^{i(\omega t - \vec k \cdot \vec x_r + \Phi)}-e^{i(\omega t - \frac{\omega L}{c} - \vec k \cdot \vec x_e + \Phi)}\right)\right] \, ,\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-255: \begin{equation}\label{eq:geo_factor_DM_GW} \mu_{re} =  \displaystyle \left(\hat n_{re} \cdot \hat e_v\right)\frac{\sqrt{16 \pi G \rho_DM}v_DM[Q_M]_d}{\omega c^2} \: \: \: \: for \:DM \: pure \: scalar \: field \, \displaystyle \left(\hat n_{re} \cdot \hat e_v \right)\frac{32 \pi G \rho_DM v_DM E^2_P[Q_M]_a}{f^2_a \omega c^3} \: \: \: \: for \: DM \: pseudo \: scalar \: field \, \displaystyle -\left(\hat n_{re} \cdot \hat e_Y \right)\frac{\sqrt{2\mu_0 \rho_DM}\epsilon e [Q_B-L]}{\omega m_TM} \: \: \: \: for \: DM \: vector \: field \, \displaystyle -\left(\frac{\hat n^i_{re}\hat n^j_{re}\hat h^SSB_{ij}(\imath, \Psi)}{2(1-\hat n_{re} \cdot \hat k)}\right) A \: \: \: \: for \: gravitational \: wave \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1093: These $\mu_{re}$ factor are the product of an amplitude and a pure geometric factor which shows how the detector couples to DM or GW, and which gives us indication on the nature of the wave.
elements.sent-2411-1094: Indeed, ULDM signal being dipolar, the LISA arm couples to a single direction (which corresponds to either the galactic velocity in the scalar cases, or to the vector polarization direction in the vector case) while GW signal being quadrupolar, the arm couples to the two orthogonal directions corresponding to the quadrupolar GW polarization.
elements.sent-2411-1095: On one hand, among other signals, LISA will be sensitive to (almost) monochromatic GW, i.e GW emitted from galactic binaries whose inspiral phase (with frequency which can be approximated as a constant) lasts longer than the total mission duration \cite{Petiteau08 .}
elements.sent-2411-1096: On the other hand, considering that the coherence time of the ULDM field exceeds the time of the mission, which we will consider to be 1 year in the following\footnote{Following Eq.~\eqref{coherence_DM , this implies that we need $f \leq 10^{-2}$ Hz.}}
elements.sent-2411-1097: This implies that both monochromatic signals from ULDM and GW Eq.~\eqref{eq:Doppler_LISA would lead to similar shapes in Fourier space (essentially one Dirac at the wave frequency).}
elements.sent-2411-1098: This suggests that the two signals might resemble each other, which could lead to degeneracy between them.
elements.sent-2411-1099: In addition, one can find simple detection setup where the quadrupolar nature of the GW signal would be seen by the detector as a dipole, therefore where the ULDM and GW signals would look similar and therefore be degenerate.
elements.sent-2411-1100: For example, let us assume a simple detection setup where the test mass $A$ is static and the test mass $B$ is located at a distance $L$ and is rotating counterclockwise around $A$ in the $x-y$ plane with angular frequency $\omega_R$.
elements.sent-2411-1101: Both test masses are sending light signals to each other.
elements.sent-2411-1102: We assume that the DM field is a pure scalar field of frequency $\omega$ whose propagation direction is along the $x$-axis in this coordinate system.
elements.sent-2411-1103: Let us further assume a monochromatic GW of the same angular frequency $\omega$ which propagates along the $y$-axis, and whose polarization is purely $+$ in the $x-z$ plane.
elements.sent-2411-1104: One can easily deduce that the GW polarization in the $z$ direction will not affect the light signals emitted between the two test masses, only the $x$ polarization will be noticed.
elements.sent-2411-1105: Therefore, the GW will be seen as a dipole by the detector.
elements.sent-2411-1106: However, in the case of a more complex detector like LISA, we might wonder if this difference in polarization would clearly lead to a different spectral shape of the signal.
elements.sent-2411-1107: In addition to this polarization difference, DM and GW propagates at different speed, i.e $|\vec k_\mathrm{GW | = \omega_\mathrm{GW /c$ while $|\vec k_\mathrm{DM | = \omega_\mathrm{DM v_\mathrm{DM /c^2 \sim 10^{-3 \omega_\mathrm{DM /c$ in Eq.~\eqref{eq:Doppler_LISA , which might also lead to a difference in the spectral signature for both signals.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1108: Note that such a difference would not be visible for time scales much smaller than one year, where the geometric factor would simply be constant.
elements.sent-2411-1109: Therefore, in the following, we will try to answer the following question : using realistic orbits, would LISA be able to break the degeneracy between GW emitted from a galactic binary and (scalar) ULDM fields ? To do so, the first step is to simulate one signal induced by a galactic binary and one signal induced by a (scalar) DM candidate.
elements.sent-2411-1110: Then, we will model one galactic binary and one scalar DM candidate using Bayesian analysis on each of these data sets.
elements.sent-2411-1111: Finally we will compare the efficiency of these models to see if, for each data set, one is preferred compared to the other.
elements.sent-2411-1112: Bayesian inference to discriminate between dark matter and gravitational waves.
elements.subsection-2411-042: A short introduction on Bayesian inference
elements.sent-2411-1113: In this section, we will review how Bayesian inference works.
elements.sent-2411-1114: It is based one Bayes' theorem, which for two random events $A$ and $B$, allows to write the probability of $A$ under the condition $B$ $P(A|B)$ as \begin{equation}\label{eq:Bayes_theorem} P(A|B) = \frac{P(A)}{P(B)}P(B|A) \, ,\end{equation} where $P(B|A)$ is the probability of $B$ under the condition $A$ and $P(A), P(B)$ respectively the probabilities of $A$ and $B$.
elements.sent-2411-1115: When considering a vector of data points $\vec d$ depending on a vector of parameters $\vec p$, Eq.~\eqref{eq:Bayes_theorem becomes \begin{equation}P\left(\vec p|\vec d\right) = \frac{L\left(\vec d|\vec p\right)\pi\left(\vec p\right)}{N\left(\vec d\right)} \, .\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-257: \begin{equation}P\left(\vec p|\vec d\right) = \frac{L\left(\vec d|\vec p\right)\pi\left(\vec p\right)}{N\left(\vec d\right)} \, .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1116: This represents what we know of the parameters, prior to looking at the data $\vec d$.
elements.sent-2411-1117: It can e.g. come from prior measurements on another set of data.
elements.sent-2411-1118: For example, the velocity distribution Eq.~\eqref{DM_vel_distrib_scalar can be used as a prior distribution for the galactic velocity (this is what will be done in the following).}
elements.sent-2411-1119: On the contrary, if no information on the given parameter is available, one might use a flat uniform prior distribution.
elements.sent-2411-1120: Therefore, in the Bayesian framework, the parameters $\vec p$ are treated as random variables and not as fixed constants.
elements.sent-2411-1121: In the most simple case, the data (of length $j$) has white noise with variance $\sigma^2$, and we model the signal as $s(\vec p)$ such that the likelihood reduces to \begin{equation}\label{eq:gaussian_likelihood} \log L\left(\vec d | \vec p\right) = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=1}^j \frac{\left(d_n-s_n(\vec p)\right)^2}{\sigma^2} \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1122: Maximizing the likelihood means minimizing the sum, which is very similar to $\chi^2$ method.
elements.sent-2411-1123: For a given set of parameters $\vec p_w$, $\mathcal{P}\left(\vec p_w | \vec d\right)\rightarrow 0$ means that $\vec p_w$ is not compatible with the data.
elements.sent-2411-1124: As the Bayes' theorem suggests it, the posterior distribution is the product of the prior distribution and the likelihood (up to $\mathcal{N (\vec d)$, a normalization constant, independent of the parameters), i.e it is the combination of our knowledge of the parameters before and after analyzing the data.}
elements.sent-2411-1125: There exists two big classes of algorithms to reconstruct the full posterior probability distribution of the parameters $\vec p$ given a dataset $\vec d$.
elements.sent-2411-1126: The first one gathers Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-1127: At each step $i$ of the algorithm, a random sample of parameters $\vec p_i$ is generated from the sample $\vec p_{i-1}$ and the acceptance of this new sample only depends on $\vec p_i$ and $\vec p_{i-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-1128: The ratio of posteriors between parameters of $i-1$ and $i$ steps is computed in order to know if newly found parameters explain the data better than the previous one.
elements.sent-2411-1129: In general, the distance in parameter space between $\vec p_i$ and $\vec p_{i-1}$ is small (compared to the parameter space volume), and therefore, the algorithm can be trapped in a local maximum, which we want to avoid.
elements.sent-2411-1130: The second method is called Nested sampling \cite{Skilling04 and is a way of avoiding being stuck in a local maximum likelihood.}
elements.sent-2411-1131: We first need to introduce the prior mass $X$ as the fraction of prior contained within an iso-likelihood contour.
elements.sent-2411-1132: Since we expect large likelihood to be contained in a small region of the parameter space, the likelihood $\mathcal{L (X)$ must be a decreasing function of $X$.}
elements.sent-2411-1133: The principle of nested sampling is to choose a set of $N$ random points covering uniformly the parameter space, and which have different likelihoods.
elements.sent-2411-1134: At the iteration $i$, one deletes the point $N_i$ with lowest likelihood $\mathcal{L (X_i)$ and finds a new point in the parameter space $N_{i+1}$ such that $\mathcal{L (X_{i+1 ) >\mathcal{L (X_{i )$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1135: This way, the remaining prior mass decreases and converges to the region with largest likelihood.
elements.sent-2411-1136: Since one can store the value of $\mathcal{L (X_i)$ at each iteration, one has access to the integral of $\mathcal{L (X)$, which corresponds to the model evidence.}}
elements.sent-2411-1137: When comparing two models, one uses the Bayes factor, which is the ratio of model evidence.
elements.sent-2411-1138: More specifically, one can write Bayes' rule in terms of $\vec p$ which depends on $\vec d$ but also on a specific model $M$, i.e \begin{equation}P(\vec p | \vec d, M) =\frac{L(\vec d | \vec p, M)\pi(\vec p | M)}{p(\vec d | M)} \, ,\end{equation} where $p(\vec d | M)$ is known as the model evidence, which is the probability of the data $\vec d$ given $M$.
elements.formula-2411-259: \begin{equation}P(\vec p | \vec d, M) =\frac{L(\vec d | \vec p, M)\pi(\vec p | M)}{p(\vec d | M)} \, ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1139: This does not depend on the parameters $\vec p$, which have been integrated out (or marginalized out).
elements.sent-2411-1140: Considering two given models $M_1$, $M_2$, the Bayes factor \begin{equation}\label{eq:Bayes_factor} B = \frac{p(\vec d | M_1)}{p(\vec d | M_2)} \, ,\end{equation} indicates which model best explain the data.
elements.sent-2411-1141: Therefore, nested sampling is very convenient to compare different models on the same data.
elements.sent-2411-1142: In the following, we will be using a nested sampler algorithm, called Nessai \cite{Nessai for the parameter estimation of both a galactic binary and a scalar ULDM field.}
elements.sent-2411-1143: Fast likelihood modeling We now focus on the analysis of TDI dataset induced by a galactic binary (GB) on one side and (scalar) ULDM on the other side.
elements.sent-2411-1144: For a given model, we want to optimize the computation of the likelihood, in particular to make it faster.
elements.sent-2411-1145: To do so, we work in the Fourier domain, because it is much more convenient for the signals that we are interested in.
elements.sent-2411-1146: Indeed, for (quasi) monochromatic signals, the idea is to separate the fast oscillation (at the wave frequency) whose Fourier transform can be done analytically, from the slowly oscillating part which can be Fourier transformed numerically \cite{Cornish07 .}
elements.sent-2411-1147: This speeds up the numerical integration necessary for the computation of the likelihood.
elements.sent-2411-1148: As we shall see in the following, the TDI combination are very simple to implement in Fourier space.
elements.sent-2411-1149: In Appendix ~\ref{ap:fastDM_fastGB , we show explicitly how to express the slow oscillating part of TDI $X_2$ combination for both scalar ULDM and GB signals.}
elements.sent-2411-1150: The two Python softwares that we use which follow this idea, are FastGB and FastDM, respectively for the modeling of GB and scalar DM.
elements.sent-2411-1151: Generation of signals and parameter space We now explicitly describe the general process from the data simulation to the data modeling.
elements.sent-2411-1152: We first simulate a dataset which contains either GB or scalar DM signal.
elements.sent-2411-1153: To do so, we simulate in time domain the second generation TDI Eq.~\eqref{eq:TDI_X_gen induced by either a GB or a scalar ULDM signals through Doppler effects derived in the previous sections, see Eqs.~\eqref{eq:Doppler_scalar_DM and \eqref{eq:Doppler_GW .}}}
elements.sent-2411-1154: The positions of the spacecrafts are obtained using LISAOrbits \cite{Bayle22 , and we use Earth-trailing orbits for their orbits \cite{Martens21 , which we simulate for one full year, i.e $T_\mathrm{obs = 365 \times 86400$ s.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1155: The positions of the spacecrafts are sampled with frequency $f_s=0.1$ Hz, i.e $N=T_\mathrm{obs f_s$ measurements are performed.}
elements.sent-2411-1156: For the generation of data, no noise is added i.e these are "perfect" dataset\footnote{Therefore, the results of the analysis, and its possible bias, will not depend on noise.. .}
elements.sent-2411-1157: For the data modeling, we fit the TDI $A,E$ data combinations expressed in Fourier domain.
elements.sent-2411-1158: More precisely, writing the TDI data in time domain $d^{A,E (t)$ as a vector $(d^{A,E _0,d^{A,E _1,...,d^{A,E _{N-1 )$ where $d^{A,E _i = d^{A,E (i/f_s)$ is the ith sampled element of $d^{A,E (t)$, we perform the discrete Fourier transform (DFT) $\tilde d^{A,E}$ defined as \begin{equation}\tilde d^{A,E}_k = \sum^{N-1}_{\ell=0} e^{-\frac{2\pi i \ell k}{N}} d^{A,E}_\ell \, ,\end{equation} and we fit a model $\tilde m^{A,E}$ to such dataset in Fourier space.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1159: We use a Gaussian likelihood expressed in time domain as \cite{Derevianko18 \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\label{eq:likelihood_time_domain_general_LISA} \log L = \sum_{x=A,E}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\log\left(\det (2\pi C^x)\right) - \frac{1}{2}\left(\vec d^x - \vec m^x\right)^\dag (C^x)^{-1}\left(\vec d^x - \vec m^x\right)\right)\, ,\end{equation} where $\vec d,\vec m$ represent respectively the time vector or data and model and where $\mathbf{C ^x$ is the noise covariance matrix of the $x$ TDI combination.}}}
elements.formula-2411-262: \begin{equation}\label{eq:likelihood_time_domain_general_LISA} \log L = \sum_{x=A,E}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\log\left(\det (2\pi C^x)\right) - \frac{1}{2}\left(\vec d^x - \vec m^x\right)^\dag (C^x)^{-1}\left(\vec d^x - \vec m^x\right)\right)\, ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1160: Using the DFT of the data and model, the likelihood can be written as \cite{Savalle21 \begin{equation}\label{eq:likelihood_general_LISA} \log L = \sum_{x=A,E}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\log\left(2\pi \Pi_{k} \tilde C^x_{kk}\right) - \sum^{N/2}_{j=0}\frac{|\tilde d^x_j- \tilde m^x_j|^2}{\tilde C^x_{jj}}\right)\, ,\end{equation} where $\tilde C^x_{kk}$ is the diagonal element at Fourier bin $k$ of the two-sided PSD matrix of the $x=A,E$ TDI combinations defined as \cite{Savalle21 \begin{equation}\tilde C^x_{kk} = N f_s \delta_{kl}N_x(f_l) \, ,\end{equation} \end{subequations where $\delta_{kl}$ is the Kronecker delta and therefore $\tilde C^x_{kk}$ is diagonal because the noise is stationary \cite{Derevianko18 , and where the $N_x(f_k)$ is the noise PSD of the second TDI $A,E$ combinations at frequency bin $k$, which are defined as \cite{Nam23 \begin{equation}\label{eq:TDI_A_noise_PSD} N_{A,E}(f) = 32\sin^2\left(\frac{2 \pi f L}{c}\right)\sin^2\left(\frac{4 \pi f L}{c}\right)\left(2\left(3+2\cos\left(\frac{2 \pi f L}{c}\right)+\cos\left(\frac{4 \pi f L}{c}\right)\right)S_acc(f)+\right.\,\nonumber \left.\left(2+\cos\left(\frac{2 \pi f L}{c}\right)\right)S_oms(f)\right) \, ,\end{equation} assuming no correlation between noises, and which is obtained by extending the single-link noise PSD Eq.~\eqref{eq:noise_PSD_LISA on the full TDI combination.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-265: \begin{equation}\label{eq:TDI_A_noise_PSD} N_{A,E}(f) = 32\sin^2\left(\frac{2 \pi f L}{c}\right)\sin^2\left(\frac{4 \pi f L}{c}\right)\left(2\left(3+2\cos\left(\frac{2 \pi f L}{c}\right)+\cos\left(\frac{4 \pi f L}{c}\right)\right)S_acc(f)+\right.\,\nonumber \left.\left(2+\cos\left(\frac{2 \pi f L}{c}\right)\right)S_oms(f)\right) \, ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1161: Since the noise PSD are equivalent for $A,E$ combinations, we can rewrite Eq.~\eqref{eq:likelihood_general_LISA as \begin{equation}\label{eq:likelihood_final_LISA} \log L = -\frac{1}{2}\log\left((2\pi)^2 f_s \Pi_{k} N_{A,E}(f_k)\right) - \sum_{x=A,E}\left(\frac{1}{N f_s}\sum^{N/2}_{j=0}\frac{|\tilde d^x_j- \tilde m^x_j|^2}{N_x(f_j)}\right) \, .\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-266: \begin{equation}\label{eq:likelihood_final_LISA} \log L = -\frac{1}{2}\log\left((2\pi)^2 f_s \Pi_{k} N_{A,E}(f_k)\right) - \sum_{x=A,E}\left(\frac{1}{N f_s}\sum^{N/2}_{j=0}\frac{|\tilde d^x_j- \tilde m^x_j|^2}{N_x(f_j)}\right) \, .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1162: The GB parameters that will be fitted (using Eqs.~\eqref{eq:alpha , \eqref{eq:mathcal_Y and \eqref{eq:beta ) are the amplitude $\mathcal{A}$, the frequency $f_\mathrm{GW}$, the drift in frequency $\dot f_\mathrm{GW}$, the sky localization ($\beta,\lambda$), the inclination $\imath$, the polarization angle $\Psi$ and the phase $\Phi_\mathrm{GW}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1163: There is no specific knowledge on the physics of one particular GB, therefore we will use isotropic prior distributions for each of those parameters.
elements.sent-2411-1164: The scalar DM parameters that will be fitted (using Eqs.~\eqref{eq:alpha_DM , \eqref{eq:mathcal_Y_DM and \eqref{eq:beta_DM ) are the mass charge\footnote{From Eq.~\eqref{partial_dil_mass_charge , we remind that $[Q_M]_d$ is a sum of partial dilatonic mass charges and dilatonic couplings.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1165: For most of these parameters, we will use isotropic priors, except for the galactic velocity amplitude prior that will be given by the amplitude of the galactic velocity distribution Eq.~\eqref{DM_vel_distrib_scalar .}
elements.sent-2411-1166: Even though we are working on time scales lower than the coherence time of the field, such that the DM wave is essentially monochromatic, the prior will help constraining the simulation to consistent regions of the parameter space\footnote{As we shall see in the following, for most frequencies under consideration, the phase shift induced by DM propagation (through $|\vec v_\mathrm{DM |$) is small, and therefore hard to fit with accuracy.. .}}
elements.sent-2411-1167: Note that, by choosing $\vec v_\mathrm{DM}$ as a random variable and not a fixed parameter, we will derive realistic sensitivity curves for ULDM of LISA.
elements.sent-2411-1168: By doing so, we simplify the system by considering that the velocity amplitude $|\vec v_\mathrm{DM |$ is uncorrelated with the DM wind direction, while it is in reality not true as both parameters are constrained together by the same distribution Eq.~\eqref{DM_vel_distrib}\footnote{As mentioned above, we will use isotropic priors for both ecliptic latitude and longitude of the DM wind, while the galactic velocity distribution Eq.~\eqref{DM_vel_distrib provides prior information on such direction.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1169: First, we make this choice because there is no analytical expression for the angular distributions only.
elements.sent-2411-1170: Second, by doing the numerical integration of Eq.~\eqref{DM_vel_distrib over the velocity amplitude, one can show that the width of the angular distributions are much larger than the posterior distributions of these parameters (see below, Section ~\ref{sec:model_DM_signal_DM ), and therefore using isotropic prior will not impact the results.. .}}
elements.subsection-2411-045: Model of galactic binary onto galactic binary signal

elements.sent-2411-1173: The yellow lines represent the true values of the parameters, i.e the values injected in the simulation.
elements.sent-2411-1174: One can notice strong correlations between the amplitude $\mathcal{A}$ and the inclination $\imath$, and between the ecliptic latitude $\beta$ and the drift in frequency $\dot f$.
elements.sent-2411-1175: One can also notice that for the polarization angle $\Psi$ and initial phase $\Phi$ parameters, two modes exist which are respectively shifted by $\pi/2$ and $\pi$.
elements.sent-2411-1176: From Eq.~\eqref{eq:Doppler_GW , it is straightforward to show that a change in polarization angle $\Psi \rightarrow \Psi + \pi/2$ is equivalent to a change in phase $\Phi \rightarrow \Phi + \pi$.}
elements.sent-2411-1177: Overall, the analysis recovers correctly the injected parameters.
elements.sent-2411-1178: Let us now analyze the result of the fit, in particular, its efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-1179: First, one can compare the signal to noise levels (i.e compute the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)) for the GW using \cite{Babak21}\footnotemark \begin{equation}\sqrt{SNR} = \sqrt{4\Re\left(\int df \frac{\tilde d_GW \tilde d^\dag_GW}{N(f)}\right)} \,,\end{equation} where $\tilde d^\dag_\mathrm{GW}$ is the conjugate transpose of $\tilde d_\mathrm{GW}$, the GW data in Fourier domain, and $N(f)$ is the noise PSD.
elements.sent-2411-1180: For the GW data from Table ~\ref{tab:GW_GW , one finds \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\sqrt{SNR^GW_A} \approx 632 \, \sqrt{SNR^GW_E} \approx 679 \, ,\end{equation} \end{subequations for both $A$ and $E$ TDI combinations.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1181: The signal power is well above the noise, as it can be noticed in Fig.~\ref{fig:GW_signal .}
elements.sent-2411-1182: On the middle, we show the GW model best fit, i.e the posterior with the largest likelihood.
elements.sent-2411-1183: The model correctly recovers the injected parameters with less than 1\% deviation.
elements.sent-2411-1184: On the right column, we present the mean and standard deviation of the full distribution of posteriors.
elements.sent-2411-1185: In a second step, in order to estimate the accuracy of the model, we simulate the TDI time series of a galactic binary with parameters given by $M^\mathrm{GW _\mathrm{GW}$, which we call $m^\mathrm{GW _\mathrm{GW}$ and we construct the residuals (in time domain) \begin{equation}r_GW,GW = d_GW - m^GW_GW \, ,\end{equation} which we compare with the LISA noise PSD.}}
elements.sent-2411-1186: In Fig.~\ref{fig:GW_signal , we compare the residuals to LISA noise in Fourier space.}
elements.sent-2411-1187: One can notice that the residuals are well below the noise, indicating that the model extracted all the necessary information out of the data\footnote{Despite its efficiency, the residuals still present a peak at the GW frequency.}
elements.sent-2411-1188: This is due to the approximations done in FastGB (e.g. neglecting the spacecraft velocity during light travel time).. .
elements.sent-2411-1189: We now compute the residuals-to-noise ratio (RNR) between the residual in Fourier domain $\tilde r_\mathrm{GW,GW}$ given by \cite{Babak21 \begin{equation}\sqrt{RNR} = \sqrt{4\Re\left(\int df \frac{\tilde r \tilde r^\dag}{N(f)}\right)} \,,\end{equation} where $\tilde r^\dag$ is the conjugate transpose of $\tilde r$.}
elements.sent-2411-1190: For the GW model on GW data, one finds \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\sqrt{RNR^GW, GW_A} \approx 0.54 \, \sqrt{RNR^GW, GW_E} \approx 0.41 \, .\end{equation}.}

elements.subsection-2411-047: Can scalar dark matter be misinterpreted as a galactic binary ?
elements.sent-2411-1218: The fitted parameters are shown in Table ~\ref{tab:GW_DM .}
elements.sent-2411-1219: We use the same procedure as before, i.e we compute the residuals between the GW model time series $m^\mathrm{GW _\mathrm{DM}$ (from the model parameters $M^\mathrm{GW _\mathrm{DM}$, see Table ~\ref{tab:GW_DM ) and the DM data $d_\mathrm{DM}$ \begin{equation}r_GW,DM = d_DM - m^GW_DM \, ,\end{equation} which allows us to compute the RNR \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\sqrt{RNR^GW, DM_A} \approx 51 \, \sqrt{RNR^GW, DM_E} \approx 52 \, .\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1220: On the middle and on the right, the GW best fit and the mean and standard deviation of the full distribution of posteriors.
elements.sent-2411-1221: One can notice different modes for $\Psi$ and $\Phi$ parameters.
elements.sent-2411-1222: Now, we wonder if LISA will be able to non ambiguously detect DM.
elements.sent-2411-1223: To do so, we use Bayesian model selection criteria which allow one to quantitatively compare the efficiency of the two different models, the GW $m^\mathrm{GW _\mathrm{DM}$ and DM $m^\mathrm{DM _\mathrm{DM}$ models, on the same dataset $d_\mathrm{DM}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1224: More precisely, we use the Bayes factor $\mathcal{B}$ which gives the ratio of the probability of getting the dataset $d_\mathrm{DM}$ under the condition of one of the model, e.g. $m^\mathrm{DM _\mathrm{DM}$, to the probability of getting $d_\mathrm{DM}$ under the condition of the second model, $m^\mathrm{GW _\mathrm{DM}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1225: This is equivalent to the ratio of the evidences (see Eq.~\ref{eq:Bayes_factor ).}
elements.sent-2411-1226: For each model, the sampler provides us the evidence of the given model.
elements.sent-2411-1227: For the dataset and models under consideration, the Bayes factor $\mathcal{B}$ is \begin{equation}\log B = \log p(d_DM|m^DM_DM) - \log p(d_DM|m^GW_DM)= O(10^3) \gg 1\, ,\end{equation} i.e given the data that contains a simulated signal, the DM model $m^\mathrm{DM _\mathrm{DM}$ is largely preferred compared to the GW model.}
elements.sent-2411-1228: Therefore, a priori, LISA is able to make the difference between one GB and scalar DM.
elements.sent-2411-1229: This suggests that if a scalar DM field exists with coupling and oscillation frequency in LISA range, LISA will be able to uncorrelate it from single galactic binaries.
elements.sent-2411-1230: In the following section, we try to answer the opposite question, namely if it is possible that a galactic binary is seen as DM by LISA, i.e if both GW and DM models efficiently fit a GB data.

elements.subsection-2411-048: Can a galactic binary be misinterpreted as scalar dark matter ?
elements.sent-2411-1231: We now focus on the opposite situation compared to the previous section, namely we use a dataset from a GB which we fit with a DM model.

elements.sent-2411-1298: The conclusions of this chapter are two-fold.
elements.sent-2411-1299: The first one is that we managed to answer the question asked at the beginning of the chapter, namely, are GB and scalar ULDM signals degenerate for LISA, i.e is it possible to model accurately a GW signal with scalar DM and vice versa ? It seems that it is not the case, i.e through a heliocentric orbit of one year, LISA is able to decorrelate the two signals.
elements.sent-2411-1300: This is encouraging with regard to the possible detection of DM by LISA.
elements.sent-2411-1301: The second conclusion is that, as we have seen in Section ~\ref{sec:model_DM_signal_DM , the fact that the ULDM field is non relativistic makes it non propagating as seen by LISA, especially at low frequency\footnote{As we shall see in Chapter ~\ref{chap:sens_experiments , even at higher frequencies, when we consider a signal strength such that SNR = 1, the velocity is still badly constrained by data.. .}}}
elements.sent-2411-1302: Therefore, the velocity of the DM wave is essentially invisible for LISA.
elements.sent-2411-1303: The amplitude of the signal being proportional to the DM-SM coupling, but also to the DM velocity, this means that LISA is in reality sensitive to the product of the two parameters, but not to one or the other individually.
elements.sent-2411-1304: In other words, if one assumes that the velocity is not fixed, but is a free parameter of the theory, the sensitivity of LISA to the coupling will decrease due to its correlation with the velocity.
elements.sent-2411-1305: In Chapter ~\ref{chap:sens_experiments , we will derive two sensitivity curves for LISA : a standard sensitivity curve where the coupling is assumed to be uncorrelated with the rest of the parameters (i.e we assume the velocity is fixed at $v_\mathrm{DM \sim 10^{-3}\: c$), and a second one, where we take into account those correlations, i.e where we keep the velocity as a free parameter.}}
elements.sent-2411-1306: The former has already been derived for one given dilaton-SM coupling in \cite{Yu23 , but the latter, which is more realistic, has never been considered in the literature.}
elements.sent-2411-1307: Search for vacuum birefringence and dichroism in LISA.
elements.figure-2411-026: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{images/LISA_bench_total.pdf} \caption{Simplified optical benches in LISA spacecrafts. The two optical benches $A$ and $B$ are inside two different spacecrafts (shown as the light green cube) and exchange light between each other. The optical elements are shown in blue, the light polarization in red and the direction of propagation with black arrows. Inside each of these optical benches, an initial "horizontal" linear polarization is produced by the laser and the association of the optical elements inside each bench fulfill all the necessary requirements for the interferometers to work (see text.).} \label{fig:opti_bench_LISA}
elements.sent-2411-1308: As we discussed it in Chapter ~\ref{axion_photon_coupling , the axion-photon coupling can be probed by its effect on polarization of light.}
elements.sent-2411-1309: In this section, we study the sensitivity of LISA to such effects.
elements.sent-2411-1310: We first describe the light polarization in LISA optical benches.
elements.sent-2411-1311: For our purposes, we are interested in the polarization of light produced and detected inside the spacecrafts and travelling in vacuum between the spacecrafts.
elements.sent-2411-1312: The current (simplified) optical bench is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:opti_bench_LISA , from \cite{Bayle23 , where we only take into account the interference between laser beams of the local spacecraft with a distant one.}}
elements.sent-2411-1313: We assume for simplicity that the light wave is produced with "horizontal" linear polarization along the $x$-axis, is travelling along the $z$ direction and arrives on the detector with "vertical" polarization after passing through the half-wave plate.
elements.sent-2411-1314: In terms of light polarization, several requirements are necessary for the detection of GW in LISA.
elements.sent-2411-1315: Each spacecraft contains two optical benches, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:opti_bench_LISA .}
elements.sent-2411-1316: Inside one given optical bench (say $A$ in spacecraft $1$), two beams are interfering : the one produced by the local laser of the given optical bench and the one which has been produced by one of the optical benches of another spacecraft ($B$ in spacecraft $2$) and which has been travelling in free space.
elements.sent-2411-1317: We require the two beams to have the same polarization, when they enter the interferometer inside optical bench $A$ (or at least that they do not have opposite linear polarizations), otherwise the interference between the two beams is zero.
elements.figure-2411-027: \begin{minipage}{\textwidth} \begin{minipage}{0.5\textwidth} As leaving one spacecraft, one small portion of the local beam can be reflected on it, come back inside and create an additional noise in the interferometer. Assuming that the local beam is at frequency $f_\ell$ while the distant beam (from the second spacecraft) has slightly different frequency $f_\ell + \delta f$, due to standard Doppler from S/C differential velocity, and frequency planning, see \cite{Heinzel24}, the reflected component of the local beam of frequency $f_\ell$ could interfere with itself but only creating a DC component. However, it could also interfere with the distant beam, and create a noise at $\delta f$. In addition, the motion of the reflecting component at the edge of the spacecraft would induce a time dependent phase on the reflected beam, which would mimic a Doppler effect induced by the GW, and therefore, this would greatly alter the measurement. To overcome this issue, we require the outgoing polarization to be different than the incoming one. Then, the polarized beam splitter selects only the polarization of interest, i.e the one of the incoming beam. It can be shown easily that the current optical benches in Fig.~\ref{fig:opti_bench_LISA} fulfill those requirements. The LISA constellation is depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:LISA_const}. Two optical benches are present in each spacecraft which permits six individual interference measurements at each time. Following our arbitrary choice on light polarization in Fig.~\ref{fig:opti_bench_LISA}, one can see that two different linear polarizations travel in space : vertical polarization travels clockwise while horizontal polarization travels counterclockwise. \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{images/LISA_constellation.pdf} \caption{LISA constellation involves three spacecrafts, denoted $1,2,3$, each containing two optical benches as described in Fig.~\ref{fig:opti_bench_LISA} denoted by "A" and "B". Six different light signals are sent between the different spacecrafts to form the interferometer; three vertically polarized beams are propagating clockwise and the other three horizontally polarized beams are propagating counterclockwise.} \label{fig:LISA_const} \end{minipage} \end{minipage}
elements.sent-2411-1318: We first study the possibility of LISA to detect axion-photon coupling through vacuum birefringence as described in Eq.~\eqref{delta_c_axion_photon .}
elements.sent-2411-1319: In the last section, we presented the standard design of LISA optical benches with linear polarized light travelling in free space.
elements.sent-2411-1320: In this section, we consider a non standard design of the optical benches to check if the search of axion-photon coupling in LISA is possible.
elements.sent-2411-1321: For this, in addition to the two previous requirements for LISA to work, we require the light polarization to be circular in free space, i.e when travelling from one spacecraft to another, in order to see any phase shift due to the long propagation between the spacecrafts.
elements.sent-2411-1322: Indeed, the axion field only impact the phase velocity of circularly polarized light and not linearly polarized one.
elements.sent-2411-1323: To do so, we will still consider an initial linear polarization but we will introduce quarter-wave plates, or other optical elements, inside the original optical benches shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:opti_bench_LISA .}
elements.sent-2411-1324: We can model the system using Jones formalism for polarization states of light and optical elements which is very convenient when treating interference phenomena \cite{Collett05 .}
elements.sent-2411-1325: This formalism discomposes the light polarization on a basis formed of the two linear polarizations.
elements.sent-2411-1326: The polarization state is modelled using a $2\times1$ matrix while the optical elements are represented by $2\times2$ matrices.
elements.sent-2411-1327: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:opti_bench_LISA , we assume the light beam to propagate along the $z$-axis with an initial polarization along the $x$-axis, that we call "horizontal" polarization.}
elements.sent-2411-1329: The other linear polarization along $y$-axis, that we will call "vertical" is represented as \begin{equation}|V\rangle =  0 \, 1  \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1330: A half-wave plate adds a phase $\phi=\pi$ to the light polarization parallel to its slow axis\footnote{The principle of a wave plate is that it presents two perpendicular directions with respective refractive index $n_f, n_s$ with $n_f<n_s$, that we call respectively fast and slow axis \cite{Hecht02 .}}
elements.sent-2411-1331: This leads to a phase shift between the light polarization along the fast axis and the one along the slow axis.. .
elements.sent-2411-1332: Its Jones matrix representation is \cite{Collett05 \begin{equation}\hat \lambda_2(\theta) = e^{-i\frac{\pi}{2}} \cos^2 \theta - \sin^2\theta 2 \sin\theta \cos \theta \, 2 \sin\theta \cos \theta \sin^2\theta-\cos^2\theta  \equiv  \cos(2\theta) \sin(2\theta) \, \sin(2\theta) -\cos(2\theta)  \, ,\end{equation} up to an irrelevant total phase, where $\theta$ is the angle between the horizontal axis (the $x$-axis) and the fast axis of the plate.}
elements.sent-2411-1333: Similarly, a quarter-wave plate introduces a phase shift of $\pi/2$ between the two linear polarized states and can be represented as \cite{Collett05 \begin{equation}\hat \lambda_4(\theta) =  \cos^2(\theta)-i\sin^2(\theta) (1+i)\sin(\theta)\cos(\theta) \, (1+i)\sin(\theta)\cos(\theta) \sin^2(\theta)-i\cos^2(\theta) \, ,\end{equation} up to a total phase.}
elements.sent-2411-1334: Note that if the half or quarter wave plate is rotated such that its fast or slow axis is along the initial linear horizontal or vertical polarization of light (i.e $\theta=0$ or $\theta=\pi/2$), there is no effect, i.e the input and output polarization are the same (up to an irrelevant phase).
elements.sent-2411-1335: Note also that the determinant of these matrices is $1$, i.e they preserve energy, or in other words, they have no absorption.
elements.sent-2411-1336: In the Jones formalism, right and left circular polarizations are represented as \cite{Collett05 \begin{subequations \begin{equation}|R\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} 1 \, i  \, |L\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} 1 \, -i  \, .\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-1337: Finally, a mirror reverses the direction of one of the two linear polarizations while letting the other one unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-1338: Its representation is \cite{Fymat71 \begin{equation}\hat M =  -1 0 \, 0 1 \, ,\end{equation} therefore it is the "horizontal" polarization that gets flipped.}
elements.sent-2411-1339: It can be shown easily that the quarter and half-wave plates commute, implying that a set of optical elements containing $n$ half-wave plates, $m$ quarter-wave plates can be described as the matrix multiplication $\hat \lambda_2^n \hat \lambda_4^m$.
elements.sent-2411-1340: However, the mirror and polarizers do not commute with the other optical elements.
elements.subsection-2411-053: Requirements for both gravitational waves and axion detection
elements.sent-2411-1341: Between the polarized beam splitter and the end of the optical bench, we will model the set of optical elements of $n$ half-wave plates, $m$ quarter-wave plates and $\ell$ polarizers (of any kind) that we add by $\hat S(\theta,\theta')$, where $\theta(\theta')$ is the angle between the fast axis of the half-wave (quarter-wave) plates and the horizontal axis.
elements.sent-2411-1342: We will denote this set of elements with superscript $A$ or $B$ for the optical bench $A$ or $B$, which are respectively associated with angles $\theta_A(\theta_B),\theta'_A(\theta'_B)$, i.e we note $\hat S^{(A) (\theta_A,\theta'_A)$ and $\hat S^{(B) (\theta_B,\theta'_B)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1343: The first requirement (noted $R_1$) is that the incoming polarization from space can interfere with the local beam, and is not fully rejected by the polarized beam splitter.
elements.sent-2411-1344: For an initial $|H\rangle$ polarization state for the beam on both spacecrafts, the polarization of the beam after leaving the optical bench $A$ and $B$ is respectively $\hat S^{(A) (\theta_A,\theta'_A)|H\rangle$ and $\hat S^{(B) (\theta_B,\theta'_B)|H\rangle$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1345: Then, after travelling in free space, each light beam enters the other spacecraft and goes through the set of optical elements but with opposite propagation direction.
elements.sent-2411-1346: In such a case, light sees the fast axis with opposite angle compared to horizontal plane such that the total polarization of the beam becomes respectively \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\label{eq:LISA_birefringence_cond1_1} \hat S^{(B)}(-\theta_B,-\theta'_B)\hat{S}^{(A)}(\theta_A,\theta'_A) |H\rangle \, \hat S^{(A)}(-\theta_A,-\theta'_A)\hat{S}^{(B)}(\theta_B,\theta'_B) |H\rangle \, .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-1347: The second requirement ($R_2$) for the optical interferometer to work is that the reflected component of light is the same as the initial one, such that it is rejected by the beam splitter, i.e we require that after travelling inside the set $\hat S(\theta,\theta')$, getting reflected on the mirror and travelling back inside $\hat S(\theta,\theta')$ (but with opposite propagation direction, as before), the polarization is "horizontal".. This means \begin{equation}\label{eq:LISA_birefringence_cond2} R_2 \Rightarrow \left\{ {rcl} \hat S^{(A)}(-\theta_A,-\theta'_A)\hat{M} S^{(A)}(\theta_A,\theta'_A) |H\rangle = |H\rangle \, \hat S^{(B)}(-\theta_B,-\theta'_B)\hat{M} S^{(B)}(\theta_B,\theta'_B) |H\rangle = |H\rangle \, ,  \right.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-1348: Finally, the additional requirement ($R_3$) to be sensitive to the axion field is that at least one of the two light polarizations in outer space has to be circularly polarized.
elements.sent-2411-1349: Following the constellation representation of Fig.~\ref{fig:LISA_const , if we assume the light propagating clockwise is right circularly polarized, then, the light propagating counterclockwise can be anything else, i.e linearly polarized or left polarized state\footnote{If it is linearly polarized or left polarized, the beatnote between the two beams will oscillate at the axion frequency, but the amplitude will be twice as big in the second case.. .}}
elements.sent-2411-1350: One can show that a solution to all these requirements is \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\hat S^{(A)} = \hat C_R \, \hat S^{(B)} = \mathds{1}_2 \, ,\end{equation} \end{subequations where the latter is the identity matrix.}}
elements.sent-2411-1351: The solution is therefore to replace the last $\hat \lambda_2$ in the optical bench $A$ (see Fig.~\ref{fig:opti_bench_LISA ), by a $\hat \lambda_4(\pi/4)\hat C_H \hat \lambda_4(-\pi/4)$ combination, i.e a first quarter-wave plate oriented with $\theta=\pi/4$, then a horizontal linear polarizer and then a second quarter wave plate oriented with $\theta=-\pi/4$, as it is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:opti_bench_LISA_modified .}}
elements.figure-2411-028: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{images/LISA_bench_total_axion_sol.pdf} \caption{Modified optical bench inside LISA spacecraft, compared to the original one presented in Fig.~\ref{fig:opti_bench_LISA}, in order to be sensitive to vacuum birefringence induced by the axion-photon coupling. The half-wave plate at the end of the optical bench $A$ is now replaced with a right polarizer which is a combination of a quarter-wave plate, a horizontal polarizer and a second quarter-wave plate, which are oriented conveniently. This configuration produces a right polarized light at the output of optical bench $A$, and fulfill all the requirements for the light interferometer to work (see text).} \label{fig:opti_bench_LISA_modified}
elements.sent-2411-1352: We show explicitly how it resolves the various requirements.
elements.sent-2411-1353: Starting with $R_3$, we have \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\hat S^{(A)} |H\rangle = |R\rangle \, \hat S^{(B)} |H\rangle = |H\rangle \neq |R\rangle \, ,\end{equation} \end{subequations and therefore, in free space, one polarization is right circularly polarized and the other linearly polarized such that the axion field impacts only one of the two beams (see Fig.~\ref{fig:opti_bench_LISA_modified ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-1354: Then, looking at $R_1$, we have \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\hat S^{(B)}\hat{S}^{(A)} |H\rangle = |R\rangle \neq |H\rangle \, \hat S^{(A)}\hat{S}^{(B)} |H\rangle = |R\rangle \neq |H\rangle \, ,\end{equation} \end{subequations i.e the light coming from the optical bench $A$ can interfere in the interferometer in optical bench $B$ and vice-versa.}}
elements.sent-2411-1355: Indeed, the light leaving optical bench $A$ is right circularly polarized, arrives in optical bench $B$ and since a circular polarization is a linear superposition of the two linear polarizations, the vertical component is reflected towards the interferometer, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:opti_bench_LISA_modified .}
elements.sent-2411-1356: The other beam coming from optical bench $B$ is in the "horizontal" polarization state in vacuum, arrives on the right polarizer of optical bench $A$, gets right circularly polarized and similarly as above, its vertical component gets reflected by the polarized beam splitter inside the interferometer (see Fig.~\ref{fig:opti_bench_LISA_modified ).}
elements.sent-2411-1357: Finally, let us make sure that no parasitic component of light gets inside the interferometers after getting reflected at the edge of the spacecraft (requirement $R_2$).
elements.sent-2411-1358: First, as we have not modified anything in the optical bench $B$, we have \begin{equation}\hat S^{(B)}\hat M \hat{S}^{(B)}|H\rangle = |H\rangle \, ,\end{equation} i.e the reflected component is in the "horizontal" polarization state, and therefore transmitted by the polarized beam splitter.
elements.sent-2411-1359: Then, it is easy to show that \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\hat C_R \hat M \hat C_R = 0 \, ,\end{equation} such that \begin{equation}\hat S^{(A)}\hat M \hat{S}^{(A)}|H\rangle = 0 \, ,\end{equation} \end{subequations i.e the entire power reflected at the edge of optical bench $A$ is absorbed by the right polarizer and therefore does not jeopardize the functioning of the interferometer.}}
elements.sent-2411-1360: This can be intuitively understood by the fact that after reflection, the right circularly polarization becomes left circularly polarized and goes back inside the right polarizer, whose role is to extract and transmit only the right polarization of the input.
elements.sent-2411-1361: Therefore, nothing from the left polarized parasitic light goes through.
elements.sent-2411-1362: In this section, we have shown that a simple modification of LISA optical benches can make it sensitive to vacuum birefringence.
elements.sent-2411-1363: Note that this modification leads to a decrease of power of light that arrives at each interferometer by a factor $4$.

elements.sent-2411-1367: Then, starting with a linear polarization (say $|H\rangle$) and adding a polarized beam-splitted such that the small $|V\rangle$ component is separated from the rest, one could detect such oscillation by photon-counting.

elements.sent-2411-1369: However, in the case of LISA, the laser power arriving at each interferometer from the distant spacecraft is small (of order of hundreds of picoWatt \cite{Petiteau08 ), and since the oscillation of direction of polarization is $\mathcal{O (g_{a\gamma ) \ll 1$, the number of photons arriving on the detection port will be extremely suppressed.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1370: In this section, we derive the Doppler effect in LISA induced by the axion-photon coupling.

elements.sent-2411-1387: In this chapter, we have seen how to search for the axion-photon coupling in LISA using its effect on birefringence and dichroism.
