elements.sent-2411-1391: General relativity and the Standard Model of particle physics are regarded today as the most successful theories in modern physics.
elements.sent-2411-1392: Despite their incredible success, their inherent formalism differences make their unification into a single theory, the theory of everything, currently impossible.
elements.sent-2411-1393: Many believe that these theories are therefore incomplete, and theorists around the world are determined to find a quantum theory of gravity.
elements.sent-2411-1394: This would certainly provide answers to unresolved mysteries arising in both theories, such as the existence (or not) of dark matter and its nature.
elements.sent-2411-1395: Indeed, while dark matter was first revealed almost a hundred years ago, its true microscopic nature is still unknown, making it one of the biggest puzzles in modern physics.
elements.sent-2411-1396: However, one must remember that it could still be an artifact of an incomplete theory of gravitation.
elements.sent-2411-1397: Among many other candidates, ultralight fields present an undeniably elegant solution to the dark matter problem.
elements.sent-2411-1398: As discussed in Chapter~\ref{DM_pheno , they induce various effects on matter, such as variation of constants of Nature, modification of classical Maxwell's equations, appearance of equivalence-principle violating accelerations, or production of electromagnetic fields.}
elements.sent-2411-1399: This kind of phenomenology can be probed accurately with quantum precision technologies, which, over the past $\sim$ thirty years, have reached unprecedented progress in the control of matter and light.
elements.sent-2411-1400: These quantum sensors include, for example, atomic clocks, electromagnetic cavities, atom interferometers, and optical interferometers.
elements.sent-2411-1401: We have seen in different parts of this thesis how each of these probes can be used to detect ultralight dark matter fields.
elements.sent-2411-1402: SYRTE, as one of the world's leading laboratories in time metrology, possesses some of the most accurate atomic clocks and atom interferometers (see e.g. \cite{Guena12,Fang16,Abgrall15,Farah14 ), and is therefore greatly involved in ultralight dark matter searches \cite{Hees16,Savalle21 .}}
elements.sent-2411-1403: SYRTE also has significant expertise in data analysis of dark matter and gravitational wave laboratory experiments.
elements.sent-2411-1404: It is in this environment that this thesis took place, making possible the investigation of many different experiments for the search of ultralight dark matter.
elements.sent-2411-1405: In terms of phenomenology, the main focus of this thesis is on 1) the violation of the equivalence principle induced by couplings between a (pseudo-)scalar dark matter field, the dilaton and the axion, and various Standard Model sectors \cite{Gue:2024onx , 2) the modification of Maxwell's equations via the introduction of a pseudo-scalar field, the axion, coupled to electromagnetism, and 3) the production of an electromagnetic field in vacuum from the coupling of a vector dark matter candidate, the dark photon, to electromagnetism \cite{Gue23,Gue:2024gws .}}
elements.sent-2411-1406: The first phenomenological aspect is related to the spacetime variation of constants of Nature, such as the fine structure constant or the electron mass, which, as a consequence, induces an oscillation of intrinsic properties of bodies, such as their rest mass, and the transition frequency of atoms.
elements.sent-2411-1407: The amplitude of such oscillations being atomic species-dependent, two bodies of distinct composition would accelerate at different rates, leading to a violation of the weak equivalence principle.
elements.sent-2411-1408: We extensively studied how MICROSCOPE, as a classical test of the equivalence principle, and atom interferometers, as its quantum equivalent, can probe dark matter fields \cite{Gue:2024onx .}
elements.sent-2411-1409: We found that MICROSCOPE could reach unprecedented sensitivity to axion-gluon coupling compared to already existing laboratory experiments, which warrants a corresponding detailed analysis of MICROSCOPE data to search for a potential signal.
elements.sent-2411-1410: Additionally, we showed that diverse atom interferometric setups, despite being similar in terms of noise levels, have contrasting sensitivities to dark matter fields.
elements.sent-2411-1411: In particular, we showed that dual-isotope interferometers, where both interferometers are spatially overlapped, have much more potential than gradiometers, where the two interferometers are stacked at different altitudes.
elements.sent-2411-1412: Even though it is natural to consider experiments involving different atomic species to detect oscillations that are species-dependent, experiments using a single species can still be competitive due to the finite speed of light.
elements.sent-2411-1413: This is the case with large space-based gravitational wave detectors, such as LISA.
elements.sent-2411-1414: Indeed, the movement of test masses caused by dark matter would generate Doppler shifts on light signals exchanged between the spacecrafts.
elements.sent-2411-1415: We investigated the possibility of the degeneracy between this signal and a monochromatic signal from a galactic binary, and if it is realistic to expect making the distinction between the two, using a Bayesian approach and a full year of realistic orbits of the spacecraft.
elements.sent-2411-1416: We found that the detector will be able to distinguish signals from scalar dark matter and monochromatic gravitational waves.
elements.sent-2411-1417: Moreover, due to the non-relativistic nature of the dark matter field, the detector cannot probe accurately its propagation velocity.
elements.sent-2411-1418: As a result, by allowing this velocity to be a free parameter in the Bayesian model, the sensitivity of the experiment to dark matter couplings decreases due to correlations.
elements.sent-2411-1419: The second phenomenological aspect of interest in this thesis is vacuum birefringence induced by the coupling between axions and photons.
elements.sent-2411-1420: Considering an electromagnetic plane wave, the axion field couples to the magnetic polarization of light and, by its inherent oscillation, causes the light polarization to oscillate as well.
elements.sent-2411-1421: If the light polarization is further decomposed into the two circular polarization states, one finds that the two states have different phase velocities, i.e., vacuum becomes birefringent.
elements.sent-2411-1422: Considering an electromagnetic cavity in which circularly polarized light is sent in, we showed that this change in phase velocity of light is, mathematically speaking, equivalent to a change in the cavity's length, leading to a phase shift of light at the output of the cavity.
elements.sent-2411-1423: We derived the sensitivity of an optical cavity and a tens-of-kilometer-long fiber link to such an effect, and we showed it is not competitive with current bounds.
elements.sent-2411-1424: We then turned to LISA again.
elements.sent-2411-1425: The current version of LISA's optical benches operates with linearly polarized light only.
elements.sent-2411-1426: We showed that a slight modification of these benches would 1) produce circularly polarized light and 2) be permitted in the setup for the search of gravitational waves.
elements.sent-2411-1427: We showed that such a modification would make LISA the most sensitive laboratory experiment to the axion-photon coupling at low masses, by five orders of magnitude.
elements.sent-2411-1428: Finally, we concentrated on laboratory probes of a small electromagnetic field induced by the kinetic mixing coupling between dark photons, a vector dark matter candidate, and photons.
elements.sent-2411-1429: This electric field oscillates at the Compton frequency of the DM field, and we have been interested in experiments probing the GHz frequency region.
elements.sent-2411-1430: We first proposed an innovative way of detecting this electric field by using Rydberg atoms inside a microwave cavity.
elements.sent-2411-1431: In short, the idea of the experiment is 1) to use the cavity as a resonator for this small electric field; 2) to inject a strong electromagnetic field inside the cavity at a close frequency from the DM Compton one, in order to produce a slowly oscillating beatnote between the two fields; 3) to use Rydberg atoms to measure this slow component by the quadratic Stark effect it produces on them.
elements.sent-2411-1432: The innovative concept of this experiment is twofold: first, the signal in our scheme is only linear in the small kinetic mixing coupling, while most experiments aim at detecting the field power, which is quadratic in the coupling; second, instead of using antennas for the measurement of the signal, we proposed to use atoms.
elements.sent-2411-1433: We showed that such an experiment would reach competitive constraints on the kinetic mixing coupling compared to already existing experiments \cite{Gue23 .}
elements.sent-2411-1434: We also studied another kind of experiment to detect this electric field, which involves a dish antenna, which reflects the small electric field and focuses it onto a horn antenna located at the curvature center of the dish \cite{Horns, SHUKET .}
elements.sent-2411-1435: On one hand, we investigated the effects of diffraction in this type of experiment from an analytical standpoint, making use of the Kirchhoff integral theorem in the low-curvature dish limit.
elements.sent-2411-1436: On the other hand, we estimated the impact of mode-matching inside the horn antenna.
elements.sent-2411-1437: We showed that the expected signal intensity can be significantly reduced compared to usual estimates.
elements.sent-2411-1438: Our method was applied to the re-interpretation of the SHUKET experiment data, the results of which were shown to be degraded by a factor of approximately fifty due to both diffraction and mode-matching \cite{Gue:2024gws .}
elements.sent-2411-1439: This analytical method allowed optimizing some experimental parameters to gain sensitivity in future runs.
elements.sent-2411-1440: In particular, following this theoretical modeling, a second run of SHUKET was recently performed, with a competitive constraint on the kinetic mixing coupling.
elements.sent-2411-1441: This thesis combines several studies that can be useful to the dark matter search community in the near future: proposals of innovative experimental schemes (Chapter ~\ref{chap:Rydberg_exp_DP ), improved modeling of already existing and future experiments (Chapters ~\ref{chap:SHUKET_exp , \ref{chap:AI , and \ref{chap:LISA_DM ), and demonstration of the potential of existing and future experiments to probe unpredicted couplings (Chapters ~\ref{chap:Classical_tests_UFF and \ref{chap:axion_photon_LISA ).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1442: It focuses on the theoretical modeling of such experiments and their corresponding sensitivity estimates, but not on the analysis of data produced by such experiments.
elements.sent-2411-1443: As a simplifying assumption, the ultralight dark matter fields are considered monochromatic, while in reality, they have a finite coherence time.
elements.sent-2411-1444: In situations where the time of observation of the experiment falls below this coherence time, they should be modeled as a stochastic superposition of plane waves oscillating at slightly different frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-1445: We believe that this should not significantly change the various sensitivity estimates made in this thesis, but as soon as data analysis is concerned, one should take this stochastic effect into account.
elements.sent-2411-1446: The outlooks opened by this thesis are large and extend from the re-analysis of existing data (such as MICROSCOPE or SHUKET), the proposal of new experiments to search for ultralight dark matter, and the development of international and futuristic projects (AION-10, MAGIS-100 and LISA) that will search for ultralight dark matter.
elements.sent-2411-1447: I genuinely enjoyed these years of PhD, and I owe it to many people I would like to thank.
elements.sent-2411-1448: I am extremely grateful to have been their PhD student and to have enjoyed their supervision.
elements.sent-2411-1449: I really appreciated their pedagogy, open-mindedness and humility, despite their experience.
elements.sent-2411-1450: Like many other scientists who know them personally, I am still amazed by their intuition, their rigor and the (very) broad spectrum of knowledge they have.
elements.sent-2411-1451: One day, I hope to become a scientist as complete as they are.
elements.sent-2411-1452: First, I would like to offer a special thanks to Marc Lilley and Robin Corgier for helping me in the preparation of my defense, but more generally for their guidance all along my PhD.
elements.sent-2411-1453: I am also thankful to Adrien Bourgoin, Samy Aoulad-Lafkih and Martin Staab for all their pieces of advice, scientifically, but not only.
elements.sent-2411-1454: Then, I would like to thank all the members of the theory and metrology team of SYRTE for their support: Pacôme Delva, Christophe Le Poncin-Lafitte, Marie-Christine Angonin, Miltos Chatzinikos and Christopher Aykroyd.
elements.sent-2411-1455: In addition, I really appreciated the help from all the people I collaborated with, and I thank them for their physical insights: Jérôme Lodewyck and Rodolphe Le Targat from SYRTE, and Etienne Savalle, Laurent Chevalier and Pierre Brun from CEA Saclay.
elements.sent-2411-1456: More generally, I thank all the people from SYRTE with whom I exchanged directly or indirectly during these three years.
elements.sent-2411-1457: I am very glad to have a jury of such quality and I can only hope to have the opportunity to discuss more with them on those dark matter searches in the future.
elements.sent-2411-1458: It has been a long journey, full of obstacles, but they always believed in me, and for this, I thank them with all my heart.. .
elements.sent-2411-1460: Phase shift induced by vacuum birefringence In this section, we explicitly derive Eq.~\eqref{eq:phase_final_DAMNED , the phase of light measured at the output of a cavity, in the framework of vacuum birefringence induced by the axion-photon coupling.}
elements.sent-2411-1461: We follow closely the derivation of the phase explicited in \cite{savallePhD .}
elements.sent-2411-1462: We start by initializing the input electric field \begin{equation}\vec \epsilon(t) = \vec \epsilon_0 e^{-i\omega_0 t} \, ,\end{equation} with amplitude $\vec \epsilon_0$ and frequency $\omega_0$.
elements.sent-2411-1463: We assume this field enters the cavity by one of the sides of the cavity.
elements.sent-2411-1464: Note that in the following, all measurable electric fields are implicitly given by the real part only.
elements.sent-2411-1465: When entering the cavity, the field gets transmitted by the cavity with a coefficient $\sqrt{1-r^2}$, where $r$ is the mirror reflectivity of the cavity and becomes \begin{equation}\vec E_0(t) = \sqrt{1-r^2} \vec \epsilon_0 e^{-i\omega_0 t} \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1466: After two reflections on the mirrors' surfaces, the electric field at the location of the first mirror is \begin{equation}\vec E_1(t) = r^2 \vec E_0(t-\tau_\leftrightarrow) = r^2 \vec E_0(t)e^{2i\frac{\omega_0}{c}\ell}e^{2i\frac{\omega_0}{c}\delta l(t-\frac{\ell}{c})}\, ,\end{equation} where we used Eq.~\eqref{eq:tau_ell to express the time variation of length of the cavity from the round trip $\tau_\leftrightarrow$.}
elements.sent-2411-1467: After $n$ round trips, the electric field inside the cavity can be written as \begin{equation}\vec E_n(t) = r^{2n} \vec E_0(t) e^{2in\frac{\omega_0}{c}\ell} e^{2i\frac{\omega_0}{c}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \delta \ell(t-(2j-1)\frac{\ell}{c})}\end{equation} at first order in the perturbation $\delta \ell(t)$.
elements.sent-2411-1468: The total electric field transmitted outside the cavity (by the cavity's opposite wall, compared to where the input electric field enters) is then \begin{equation}\vec E^transmit_tot(t) = \sum^\infty_{n=0} E^transmit_n (t)= \frac{\sqrt{1-r^2} \vec E_0(t)}{r^2}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}r^{2n}e^{2in\frac{\omega_0}{c}\ell}e^{2i\frac{\omega_0}{c}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\delta \ell^{(j)}} \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1469: Since we are interested in the field transmitted to the second mirror (not the one from which the field initially enters the cavity), we explicitly add a factor $\sqrt{1-r^2}$ to account for the transmission to the second mirror and we remove a factor $r^2$, since in that case, the field is not reflected on the second mirror and then on the first one on his way back.
elements.sent-2411-1470: This is also why we sum over the fields from $n=1$.
elements.sent-2411-1472: We now use the fact that the parameter $\delta \ell^{(j)}$ is small ($\mathcal{O (g_{a\gamma}$)), allowing us to expand the exponential at first order in $\delta \ell^{(j)}$, i.e \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}r^{2n}e^{2in\frac{\omega_0}{c}\ell}e^{2i\frac{\omega_0}{c}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\delta \ell^{(j)}}=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}r^{2n}e^{2ink_0\ell}+2ik_0\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}r^{2n}e^{2ink_0\ell}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\delta \ell^{(j)} \, = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}r^{2n}e^{2ink_0\ell}+2ik_0\left(\delta \ell^{(1)}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}r^{2n}e^{2ink_0\ell}+\delta\ell^{(2)}\sum_{n=2}^{\infty}r^{2n}e^{2ink_0\ell}+...\right) \, = \left(r^2e^{2ik_0\ell}+2ik_0r^2e^{2ik_0\ell}\delta\ell^{(1)}+2ik_0r^4e^{4ik_0\ell}\delta\ell^{(2)}+...\right)\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}r^{2n}e^{2ink_0\ell}\, =r^2e^{2ik_0\ell}\left(1+\frac{2ik_0}{r^2}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\delta \ell^{(n)}r^{2n}\right)\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}r^{2n}e^{2ink_0\ell}\, =\frac{r^2e^{2ik_0\ell}}{1-e^{2ik_0\ell}r^2}e^{\frac{2ik_0}{r^2}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\delta \ell^{(n)}r^{2n}}\end{equation} \end{subequations where $k_0=\omega_0/c$ and where at the last line, we inverted the Taylor expansion, such that the total transmitted electric field becomes \begin{equation}\vec E^transmit_tot(t) = \frac{\sqrt{1-r^2} \vec E_0(t) e^{2ik_0\ell}}{1-e^{2ik_0\ell}r^2}e^{\frac{2ik_0}{r^2}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\delta \ell^{(n)}r^{2n}}\end{equation} We recover the usual form of electromagnetic plane wave where the amplitude $\vec A(t)$ and phase $\phi(t)$ are respectively given by \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\vec A(t) = \frac{(1-r^2) e^{2ik_0\ell}}{1-e^{2ik_0\ell}r^2}\vec \epsilon_0 \, \phi(t) =\omega_0 t - \frac{2k_0}{r^2}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\delta \ell^{(n)}r^{2n} \, .\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1473: Using Eq.~\eqref{delta_l , the phase shift between the transmitted field and the input oscillates at the axion frequency $\omega_a$ whose amplitude is \begin{subequations \begin{equation}|\Delta \phi(t)| = \left|\frac{2 k_0}{r^2} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}r^{2n}\delta \ell^{(n)}\right|\, =\frac{2\sqrt{16\pi G \rho_DM} E_P g_{a\gamma}}{\omega_a c}\sin^2\left(\frac{\omega_a \ell}{2c}\right)\frac{\left|\cos(\omega_a t-\frac{2\omega_a \ell}{c}+\Phi)-r^2\cos(\omega_a t+\Phi)\right|}{1-2r^2\cos\left(\frac{2\omega_a \ell}{c}\right)+r^4}\, \approx \frac{2\sqrt{16\pi G \rho_DM} E_P g_{a\gamma}}{\omega_a c\sqrt{1-2r^2\cos\left(\frac{2\omega_a \ell}{c}\right)+r^4}}\sin^2\left(\frac{\omega_a \ell}{2c}\right) \, .\end{equation}. \end{subequations.}}}
elements.formula-2411-319: \begin{equation}|\Delta \phi(t)| = \left|\frac{2 k_0}{r^2} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}r^{2n}\delta \ell^{(n)}\right|\, =\frac{2\sqrt{16\pi G \rho_DM} E_P g_{a\gamma}}{\omega_a c}\sin^2\left(\frac{\omega_a \ell}{2c}\right)\frac{\left|\cos(\omega_a t-\frac{2\omega_a \ell}{c}+\Phi)-r^2\cos(\omega_a t+\Phi)\right|}{1-2r^2\cos\left(\frac{2\omega_a \ell}{c}\right)+r^4}\, \approx \frac{2\sqrt{16\pi G \rho_DM} E_P g_{a\gamma}}{\omega_a c\sqrt{1-2r^2\cos\left(\frac{2\omega_a \ell}{c}\right)+r^4}}\sin^2\left(\frac{\omega_a \ell}{2c}\right) \, .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1474: Amplitude of the square of the total electric field inside a cavity In this appendix, we derive the amplitude of the electric field power inside a cavity Eq.~\eqref{eq:Etot2 in the context of the experiment presented in Chapter ~\ref{chap:Rydberg_exp_DP .}}
elements.sent-2411-1475: From Eq.~\eqref{general_E_power , we can write the signal amplitude as \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\sqrt{\left(\vec A(\omega_A)\cdot \vec C(\omega_U)+\vec B(\omega_A)\cdot \vec D(\omega_U)\right)^2+{\left(\vec B(\omega_A)\cdot \vec C(\omega_U)-\vec A(\omega_A)\cdot \vec D(\omega_U)\right)^2}}\, \equiv X_A X_DM\beta\sqrt{\left( A'(\omega_A)^2+B'(\omega_A)^2\right)\times\left(C'(\omega_U)^2+ D'(\omega_U)^2\right)}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\beta = \hat e_DM \cdot \frac{\vec X_A}{X_A} \, ,\end{equation} \end{subequations is the projection of the polarization of the DP field on the polarization of the injected electric field, such that $\vec X_A \cdot \vec X_\mathrm{DM = \vec X_A \cdot \vec X_\mathrm{DM,\parallel = X_A X_\mathrm{DM \beta$ and where the prime quantities are defined as \begin{subequations \begin{equation}A'(\omega_A) \equiv \frac{\sqrt{1-r^2}\left(1+r\right)\cos\left(\frac{\omega_AL}{2c}\right)}{1+2r\cos(\frac{\omega_AL}{c})+r^2}\, , B'(\omega_A) \equiv \frac{\sqrt{1-r^2}\left(1-r\right)\sin\left(\frac{\omega_AL}{2c}\right)}{1+2r\cos(\frac{\omega_AL}{c})+r^2}\, , C'(\omega_U) \equiv 1 +\frac{2(1+r)\cos(\frac{\omega_U L}{2c})}{1+2r\cos(\frac{\omega_U L}{c})+r^2}\, , D'(\omega_U) \equiv \frac{2(1-r)\sin(\frac{\omega_U L}{2c})}{1+2r\cos(\frac{\omega_U L}{c})+r^2}\, ,\end{equation} \end{subequations i.e the polarizations are factorized from the amplitude functions $\{\vec A, \vec B, \vec C, \vec D\}$.}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-320: \begin{equation}\sqrt{\left(\vec A(\omega_A)\cdot \vec C(\omega_U)+\vec B(\omega_A)\cdot \vec D(\omega_U)\right)^2+{\left(\vec B(\omega_A)\cdot \vec C(\omega_U)-\vec A(\omega_A)\cdot \vec D(\omega_U)\right)^2}}\, \equiv X_A X_DM\beta\sqrt{\left( A'(\omega_A)^2+B'(\omega_A)^2\right)\times\left(C'(\omega_U)^2+ D'(\omega_U)^2\right)}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-322: \begin{equation}A'(\omega_A) \equiv \frac{\sqrt{1-r^2}\left(1+r\right)\cos\left(\frac{\omega_AL}{2c}\right)}{1+2r\cos(\frac{\omega_AL}{c})+r^2}\, , B'(\omega_A) \equiv \frac{\sqrt{1-r^2}\left(1-r\right)\sin\left(\frac{\omega_AL}{2c}\right)}{1+2r\cos(\frac{\omega_AL}{c})+r^2}\, , C'(\omega_U) \equiv 1 +\frac{2(1+r)\cos(\frac{\omega_U L}{2c})}{1+2r\cos(\frac{\omega_U L}{c})+r^2}\, , D'(\omega_U) \equiv \frac{2(1-r)\sin(\frac{\omega_U L}{2c})}{1+2r\cos(\frac{\omega_U L}{c})+r^2}\, ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1476: The signal amplitude can be easily simplified to \begin{equation}\frac{\sqrt{1-r^2}X_AX_DM\beta}{\sqrt{1+2r\cos(\frac{\omega_A L}{c})+r^2}}\sqrt{1+4\frac{1+(1+r)\cos(\frac{\omega_U L}{2c})}{1+2r\cos(\frac{\omega_U L}{c})+r^2}} \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1477: Amplitude fluctuation inside a cavity In this section, we show how to express the contribution in field power from the amplitude fluctuation of the applied field $\Delta X_A$ Eq.~\eqref{general_E_power_noise .}
elements.sent-2411-1478: We use the same procedure as the one described in Section ~\ref{Total_E_field_DP to compute the RIN contribution to the total electric field at the center of the cavity.}
elements.sent-2411-1479: Starting from its expression in Eq.~\eqref{stochastic_noise_single , the first contribution of noise reads \begin{equation}\left[\vec E^0_A\right]_RIN(x=0,t) = \Re \left[\sqrt{1-r^2}\left(\frac{\Delta \vec{X}_A(\Delta \omega)}{2}\left(e^{-i(\omega_+t-k_+\frac{L}{2}+\phi_+)}+e^{-i(\omega_-t-k_-\frac{L}{2}+\phi_-)}\right)\right)\right]\, ,\end{equation} where we used the trigonometric identity $2\cos(A)\cos(B) = \cos(A+B)+\cos(A-B)$.}
elements.sent-2411-1480: Here, the only angular frequency noise we are interested in is $\omega_0 = \Delta \omega$, hence $k_\pm = k_A \pm \Delta k$, $\Delta k = \Delta \omega/c$ and $\Delta \vec X_A$ is evaluated at $\Delta \omega$.
elements.sent-2411-1481: After getting reflected on the other boundary, the second contribution is \begin{equation}\left[\vec E^1_A\right]_RIN(x=0,t) = \Re\left[-\sqrt{1-r^2}re^{ik_AL}\left(\frac{\Delta \vec{X}_A(\Delta \omega)}{2}\left(e^{-i(\omega_+t-k_+\frac{L}{2}+\phi_+)}+e^{-i(\omega_-t-k_-\frac{L}{2}+\phi_-)}\right)\right)\right]\, ,\end{equation} and after an infinite number of round trips N, the full RIN contribution on the electric field inside the cavity is \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\left[\vec E^tot_A\right]_RIN(x=0,t) = \sum_{n=0}^{N=+\infty} \vec E^{n}_RIN(x=0,t) \, =\sqrt{1-r^2}\Delta \vec X_A(\Delta \omega)\left(\Re \left[e^{-i(\omega_+t+\phi_+)}\frac{e^{i\frac{k_+L}{2}}}{1+re^{ik_+L}} + e^{-i(\omega_-t+\phi_-)}\frac{e^{i\frac{k_-L}{2}}}{1+re^{ik_-L}}\right]\right)\, = \frac{\Delta X_A(\Delta \omega)}{2 X_A}\sum_{i=\pm}\left(\vec A(\omega_i)\cos(\omega_it+\phi_i)+\vec B(\omega_i)\sin(\omega_it+\phi_i)\right) \, =\frac{\Delta X_a(\Delta \omega)}{2X_A}\left( \vec A(2\omega_A-\omega_U)\cos([2\omega_A-\omega_U]t+\phi_+) +\vec A(\omega_U)\cos(\omega_U t+\phi_-) +\nonumber \right. \left.\vec B(2\omega_A-\omega_U)\sin([2\omega_A-\omega_U]t+\phi_+) +\vec B(\omega_U)\sin(\omega_U t+\phi_-)\right)\end{equation} \end{subequations where the functions $\vec A$ and $\vec B$ have already been defined in Eqs.~\eqref{eq:A_B and where we used $\omega_\pm = \omega_A \pm \Delta \omega$ at the last line, and where $\phi_\pm=\phi_A \pm\phi_0$.}}}
elements.formula-2411-326: \begin{equation}\left[\vec E^tot_A\right]_RIN(x=0,t) = \sum_{n=0}^{N=+\infty} \vec E^{n}_RIN(x=0,t) \, =\sqrt{1-r^2}\Delta \vec X_A(\Delta \omega)\left(\Re \left[e^{-i(\omega_+t+\phi_+)}\frac{e^{i\frac{k_+L}{2}}}{1+re^{ik_+L}} + e^{-i(\omega_-t+\phi_-)}\frac{e^{i\frac{k_-L}{2}}}{1+re^{ik_-L}}\right]\right)\, = \frac{\Delta X_A(\Delta \omega)}{2 X_A}\sum_{i=\pm}\left(\vec A(\omega_i)\cos(\omega_it+\phi_i)+\vec B(\omega_i)\sin(\omega_it+\phi_i)\right) \, =\frac{\Delta X_a(\Delta \omega)}{2X_A}\left( \vec A(2\omega_A-\omega_U)\cos([2\omega_A-\omega_U]t+\phi_+) +\vec A(\omega_U)\cos(\omega_U t+\phi_-) +\nonumber \right. \left.\vec B(2\omega_A-\omega_U)\sin([2\omega_A-\omega_U]t+\phi_+) +\vec B(\omega_U)\sin(\omega_U t+\phi_-)\right)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1482: This expression has to be directly added to the noise-less contribution from Eq.~\eqref{full_field_applied_simplified .}
elements.sent-2411-1483: Then, the RIN contribution to the square of the total electric field at the center of the cavity can be obtained by multiplying the last equation with Eq.~\eqref{full_field_applied_simplified , as we work at first order in $\Delta X_A/X_A$.}
elements.sent-2411-1484: Keeping only the terms oscillating at angular frequency $\Delta \omega$, the RIN contribution to $E^2$ is given by \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\left[E^2(\omega_U,\omega_A)\right]_RIN=\frac{\Delta X_A(\Delta \omega)}{2X_A}\left(\left(A(\omega_A)\left[A(2\omega_a-\omega_U)+A(\omega_U)\right]+B(\omega_A)\left[B(2\omega_A-\omega_U)+B(\omega_U)\right]\right)\right.\nonumber \left.\cos(\Delta \omega t+\phi_0) +\left(A(\omega_A)\left[B(2\omega_A-\omega_U)-B(\omega_U)\right]+B(\omega_A)\left[A(\omega_U)-A(2\omega_A-\omega_U)\right]\right)\sin(\Delta \omega t+\phi_0)\right) \label{full_E_power_noise} = \frac{\Delta X_A (\Delta \omega)}{2X_A}\sqrt{N(\omega_U,\omega_A)} \cos(\Delta \omega t + \varphi)= \sqrt{\frac{P_RIN N(\omega_U,\omega_A)}{8 T_obs \Delta \omega}} \cos(\Delta \omega t + \varphi)\, ,\end{equation} \end{subequations where we have used Eqs.~\eqref{eq:PSD_noise and \eqref{eq:flicker_noise .}}}}
elements.formula-2411-327: \begin{equation}\left[E^2(\omega_U,\omega_A)\right]_RIN=\frac{\Delta X_A(\Delta \omega)}{2X_A}\left(\left(A(\omega_A)\left[A(2\omega_a-\omega_U)+A(\omega_U)\right]+B(\omega_A)\left[B(2\omega_A-\omega_U)+B(\omega_U)\right]\right)\right.\nonumber \left.\cos(\Delta \omega t+\phi_0) +\left(A(\omega_A)\left[B(2\omega_A-\omega_U)-B(\omega_U)\right]+B(\omega_A)\left[A(\omega_U)-A(2\omega_A-\omega_U)\right]\right)\sin(\Delta \omega t+\phi_0)\right) \label{full_E_power_noise} = \frac{\Delta X_A (\Delta \omega)}{2X_A}\sqrt{N(\omega_U,\omega_A)} \cos(\Delta \omega t + \varphi)= \sqrt{\frac{P_RIN N(\omega_U,\omega_A)}{8 T_obs \Delta \omega}} \cos(\Delta \omega t + \varphi)\, ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1485: In this expression, the functions $A$ and $B$ are the norm of $\vec A$ and $\vec B$ and $\varphi$ is an irrelevant phase dependent on $A$ and $B$.
elements.sent-2411-1486: The function $\sqrt{N(\omega_U,\omega_A)}$ correspond to the noise amplification factor by the cavity, quadratic in $X_A$ and whose expression is given by \begin{subequations \begin{equation}N(\omega_U,\omega_A) \equiv N_1(\omega_U,\omega_A)+N_2(\omega_U,\omega_A)+N_3(\omega_U,\omega_A)\, ,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}N_1(\omega_U,\omega_A) \equiv A(\omega_A)^2\left(\left(A(2\omega_A-\omega_U)+A(\omega_U)\right)^2+\left(B(2\omega_A-\omega_U)-B(\omega_U)\right)^2\right)\, , N_2(\omega_U,\omega_A) \equiv B(\omega_A)^2\left(\left(A(2\omega_A-\omega_U)-A(\omega)\right)^2+\left(B(2\omega_A-\omega_U)+B(\omega_U)\right)^2\right)\, , N_3(\omega_U,\omega_A) \equiv 4A(\omega_A)B(\omega_A)\left(A(2\omega_A-\omega_U)B(\omega_U)+B(2\omega_A-\omega_U)A(\omega_U)\right)\, . \label{noise_amp}\end{equation} \end{subequations.}}
elements.sent-2411-1487: Motion of atomic wavepackets inside an atom interferometer In this appendix, we compute rigorously the wavepackets position and velocity along the $\pi/2-\pi-\pi/2$ interferometer, which will be used for the computation of the phase shift observable in Chapter ~\ref{chap:AI .}
elements.sent-2411-1488: We start by the calculation of the position and velocity of the atom at the end of the trajectory portion 1 in Fig.~\ref{Mach-Zehnder_perturbed .}
elements.sent-2411-1489: From Eq.~\eqref{EoM_atom_AI , the atom does not undergo any kick velocities from the laser pulses, hence after a time T, its equations of motion read \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\vec x^{(1)}_A(t=T) = \vec v_DM\left(T-\frac{[Q^A_M]_d X_DM}{\omega_\phi}[\sin(\omega_\phi T+\Phi)-\sin(\Phi)- \omega_\phi T\cos(\Phi)]\right)\, \vec v^{(1)}_A(t=T) = \vec v_DM\left(1-[Q^A_M]_d X_DM\left[\cos(\omega_\phi T+\Phi)-\cos(\Phi)\right]\right) \, .\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-330: \begin{equation}\vec x^{(1)}_A(t=T) = \vec v_DM\left(T-\frac{[Q^A_M]_d X_DM}{\omega_\phi}[\sin(\omega_\phi T+\Phi)-\sin(\Phi)- \omega_\phi T\cos(\Phi)]\right)\, \vec v^{(1)}_A(t=T) = \vec v_DM\left(1-[Q^A_M]_d X_DM\left[\cos(\omega_\phi T+\Phi)-\cos(\Phi)\right]\right) \, .\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-1494: Projection of galactic velocity onto sensitive axes of various experiments In this appendix, we will compute the factors that are orientation dependent in the various acceleration or phase shifts observables, presented in Chapters ~\ref{chap:Classical_tests_UFF and \ref{chap:AI .}}
elements.sent-2411-1495: The observable signatures depend on the orientation of the experimental setup with respect to the initial velocity of the atoms or tests masses, which corresponds at leading order to the galactic velocity $v_\mathrm{DM}$.
elements.sent-2411-1496: Indeed, for classical tests of the universality of free fall between two macroscopic bodies, one measures $\Delta \vec a \propto \vec v_\mathrm{DM}$ (see Eq.\eqref{delta_a_UFF_osc ) projected onto the sensitive axis of the instrument.}
elements.sent-2411-1497: Therefore, the signal depends on the projection of the velocity of the bodies in the galactocentric frame with the sensitive axis of the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-1498: Similarly, all the AI experiments depend on the scalar product between the initial galactocentric velocity of the atomic clouds and the direction of the velocity kick undergone in the interferometric schemes, i.e. on $\hat e_v \cdot \hat e_\mathrm{kick}$, see Eqs.~\eqref{eq:MZ_delta_phase_shift and \eqref{eq:phase_new_prop .}}
elements.sent-2411-1499: As mentioned in Chapter ~\ref{chap:LISA_DM , the direction of the DM velocity in the galactic halo points towards $\alpha_\mathrm{DM ,\delta_\mathrm{DM =310.36\degree \mathrm{E ,45.28\degree \mathrm{N}$ \cite{vanLeeuwen07 in the equatorial frame.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1500: All the AI-based experiments operate at constant location, loc, on Earth with longitude $\lambda_\mathrm{loc}$ and latitude $\phi_\mathrm{loc}$.
elements.sent-2411-1501: We assume the velocity kick to be directed vertically, i.e. in the Earth's reference frame $\hat e_\mathrm{kick =\left(\cos (\lambda_\mathrm{loc )\cos (\phi_\mathrm{loc ),\sin (\lambda_\mathrm{loc )\cos (\phi_\mathrm{loc ),\sin (\phi_\mathrm{loc )\right)$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1502: As first approximation, we consider the declination as equivalent to the terrestrial latitude, i.e $\delta_\mathrm{DM}\approx \phi_\mathrm{DM}$, such that the dot product $\hat e_v \cdot \hat e_\mathrm{kick}$ is simply given by $\cos(\phi_\mathrm{loc )\cos(\phi_\mathrm{DM )\cos(\lambda_\mathrm{loc -\lambda_\mathrm{DM (t))+\sin(\phi_\mathrm{loc )\sin(\phi_\mathrm{DM )$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1503: Indeed, while $\alpha_\mathrm{DM}$ is fixed, the former follows the Earth rotation with frequency $\omega_E \sim 7 \times 10^{-5}$ Hz and is therefore time dependent \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\lambda_DM(t) =\omega_E t+\varphi \, ,\end{equation} where the phase $\varphi$ corresponds to the longitude of $\alpha$ Cygni at the origin of time reference considered.}
elements.sent-2411-1504: For short experiments with an experimental time much smaller than a day (i.e. relevant for Stanford's experiment, see Sec~\ref{sec:stanford ), the dot product is roughly constant and depends therefore on the exact time of the day when the experiment was conducted.}
elements.sent-2411-1505: In order to infer a sensitivity estimate, we will only consider the mean value of the dot product which is given by $\sin(\phi_\mathrm{loc )\sin(\phi_\mathrm{DM )$, i.e \begin{equation}\label{dot_product_DM_axis_1} \hat e_v \cdot \hat e_kick\Big|_Stanford \approx 0.43 \, .\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-1506: For longer time experiments, like the SPID variation, the dot product evolves with time, such that the signal evolves as \begin{equation}s(t) \propto \hat e_v \cdot \hat e_kick \cos(\omega_DMt +\Phi)= [\cos(\phi_loc)\cos(\phi_DM)\cos(\omega_E t +\varphi)+\,\nonumber \sin(\phi_loc)\sin(\phi_DM)] \cos(\omega_DMt +\Phi) ,\end{equation} i.e the Earth rotation modulates the signal at frequency $f_E=\omega_E/2\pi$.
elements.sent-2411-1507: We will make two approximations for our estimates.
elements.sent-2411-1508: For all the DM frequencies of interest $\omega_\mathrm{DM}$, the Earth rotation is a slowly varying function, because $\omega_E < \omega_\mathrm{DM}$.
elements.sent-2411-1509: Therefore, we will assume that the signal will manifest itself by a single peak at frequency $f_\mathrm{DM = \omega_\mathrm{DM /2\pi$ in Fourier space.}}
elements.sent-2411-1510: In addition, we will be interested only by the maximum value of the dot product, which is only a function of the different latitudes.. \begin{equation}\hat e_v \cdot \hat e_kick = Max\left(|\cos(\phi_loc\pm \phi_DM)|\right) \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1511: For the various locations under consideration in this thesis, we have \begin{equation}\label{dot_product_DM_axis_2} \hat e_v \cdot \hat e_kick\Big|_Oxford \approx 0.99 \, , \hat e_v \cdot \hat e_kick\Big|_Fermilab \approx 1.00 \, ,\end{equation} where we assume the SPID variation operates at the same location as AION-10, i.e Oxford \cite{Badurina20 for consistent comparison.}
elements.sent-2411-1512: For MICROSCOPE, the axis of measurement is alongside the test masses cylinders' longitudinal symmetry axis \cite{Microscope17 .}
elements.sent-2411-1513: The orbital motion of the satellite around Earth is sun-synchronous, which means that the orientation of the orbital plane evolves with time with an annual period.
elements.sent-2411-1514: As it was mentioned in Section ~\ref{sec:exp_param_MICROSCOPE , the measurements are distributed on 17 different sessions, each of them lasting $T_\mathrm{int /17 \sim 5$ days on average.}}
elements.sent-2411-1515: For such duration, we can assume the orbital plane to be fixed during each session.
elements.sent-2411-1516: In addition, the satellite spins around an axis that is orthogonal to the axis of measurement with angular frequency $\omega_\mathrm{spin}$.
elements.sent-2411-1517: In total, this means the dot product can be written as \begin{equation}\hat e_v \cdot \hat e_meas.(t)\Big|_\mu SCOPE = A(t) \cos(\omega_spin t + \psi) \, ,\end{equation} where $A(t)$ depends on the orientation of the orbital plane, i.e is fixed for one session but changes from one session to another, and with $\psi$ an irrelevant phase.
elements.sent-2411-1518: Using MICROSCOPE's publicly available data, we estimated numerically the coefficient $A(t)$ for every session and we find that its value oscillates between 0.71 and 1.
elements.sent-2411-1519: For our estimates, we will consider its mean value, i.e $|\hat e_v \cdot \hat e_\mathrm{meas.. | \approx 0.85$.}
elements.sent-2411-1520: In conclusion, the signal presented in Eq.~\eqref{delta_a_UFF_osc is modulated by the factor \begin{equation}\hat e_v \cdot \hat e_meas.(t)\Big|_\mu SCOPE \approx 0.85 \cos(\omega_spin t + \psi) \label{dot_product_DM_MICRO} \, .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-1521: Therefore, for DM frequencies in Eq.~\eqref{delta_a_UFF_osc such that $\omega_\mathrm{DM \ll \omega_\mathrm{spin}$, we will consider that the signal in MICROSCOPE oscillates at $\omega_\mathrm{spin}$, whereas if $\omega_\mathrm{DM \gg \omega_\mathrm{spin}$, we will make our estimate with the signal oscillating at $\omega_\mathrm{DM}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1522: As explained in Section ~\ref{sec:exp_param_MICROSCOPE , $\omega_\mathrm{spin}$ depends on the measurement session since three different spinning frequencies have been used during the full mission.}
elements.sent-2411-1523: They differ by a factor 5 roughly \cite{Berge23 .}
elements.sent-2411-1524: For our rough sensitivity estimates, we use the data from session 404 as a basis, therefore we will assume a constant $\omega_\mathrm{spin \sim 18.4$~mrad/s.}
elements.sent-2411-1525: Fast likelihood for scalar ultralight dark matter In this appendix, we derive (in time domain) the slowly oscillating component of TDI $X$ combination of a scalar ULDM signal, that will be used for the DM model in FastDM in Section ~\ref{sec:ULDM_GW_discriminate .}
elements.sent-2411-1526: We will make the detailed calculations for a (pure scalar) DM signal, and then we generalize to GW.
elements.sent-2411-1527: Let us start again with the one-link response function induced by scalar DM Eq.~\eqref{eq:Doppler_scalar_DM at time of reception of the photon of the receiver $r$ $t_r$, when it is emitted at time $t_e=t_r-L/c$ by emitter $e$ \begin{subequations \begin{equation}y^DM_{re}(t_r) = \left(\hat n_{re} \cdot \hat e_v\right)\frac{\sqrt{16 \pi G \rho_DM}v_DM[Q_M]_d}{\omega_\phi c^2}\Re\left[e^{i\phi_r}- e^{i\phi_e}\right] \, =\left(\hat n_{re} \cdot \hat e_v\right)\frac{\sqrt{16 \pi G \rho_DM}v_DM[Q_M]_d}{\omega_\phi c^2}\Re\left[e^{i\phi_r}\left(1- e^{-2i\alpha^DM_{re}}\right)\right]\, =2\left(\hat n_{re} \cdot \hat e_v\right)\frac{\sqrt{16 \pi G \rho_DM}v_DM[Q_M]_d}{\omega_\phi c^2}\sin(\alpha^DM_{re})\Re\left[ie^{i\phi_r}e^{-i\alpha^DM_{re}}\right]\, \equiv Y_{re}(t_r) e^{i\phi_r} \, ,\end{equation} \end{subequations where \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\phi_r = \omega_\phi t_r- \vec k_\phi \cdot \vec x_r(t_r) + \Phi\, \phi_e = \omega_\phi t_e -\vec k_\phi \cdot \vec x_e(t_e) + \Phi \, ,\end{equation} and therefore \begin{equation}2\alpha^DM_{re}(t_r) = \phi_r-\phi_e = \omega_\phi\left((t_r-t_e)-\frac{\hat k \cdot \hat n_{re} |\vec v_DM|}{c^2}\left|\vec x_r(t_r)-\vec x_e(t_e)\right|\right) \, =\frac{\omega_\phi \left|\vec x_r(t_r)-\vec x_e(t_e)\right|}{c}\left(1 - \frac{\hat k \cdot \hat n_{re} |\vec v_DM|}{c}\right) + \mathcal O(Shapiro) \, ,\end{equation} \end{subequations where we neglect the impact of the Shapiro delay on the signal.}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-342: \begin{equation}y^DM_{re}(t_r) = \left(\hat n_{re} \cdot \hat e_v\right)\frac{\sqrt{16 \pi G \rho_DM}v_DM[Q_M]_d}{\omega_\phi c^2}\Re\left[e^{i\phi_r}- e^{i\phi_e}\right] \, =\left(\hat n_{re} \cdot \hat e_v\right)\frac{\sqrt{16 \pi G \rho_DM}v_DM[Q_M]_d}{\omega_\phi c^2}\Re\left[e^{i\phi_r}\left(1- e^{-2i\alpha^DM_{re}}\right)\right]\, =2\left(\hat n_{re} \cdot \hat e_v\right)\frac{\sqrt{16 \pi G \rho_DM}v_DM[Q_M]_d}{\omega_\phi c^2}\sin(\alpha^DM_{re})\Re\left[ie^{i\phi_r}e^{-i\alpha^DM_{re}}\right]\, \equiv Y_{re}(t_r) e^{i\phi_r} \, ,\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-1528: The expression of $\alpha^\mathrm{DM _{re}$ is therefore given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:alpha_DM} \alpha^\mathrm{DM}_{re}(t_r) =\frac{\omega_\phi \left|\vec x_r(t_r)-\vec x_e(t_e)\right|}{2c}\left(1 - \frac{\hat k \cdot \hat n_{re} |\vec v_\mathrm{DM}|}{c}\right) \, .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-1529: Let us now decompose the one-link response function into a slowly evolving part and a fast oscillating part.
elements.sent-2411-1530: Let us consider the observation time baseline $T_\mathrm{obs}$ such that the corresponding Fourier frequencies are given by integer times $1/T_\mathrm{obs}$.
elements.sent-2411-1531: Let us consider the Fourier frequency $q_0$ which is the closest to $f_\phi$, i.e. \begin{equation}\label{eq:q} q_0= \mathrm{round}\left[f_\phi T_\mathrm{obs}\right]/T_\mathrm{obs} \,\end{equation} where $\mathrm{round }\left[x\right]$ provides the closest integer to $x$.
elements.sent-2411-1532: One can rewrite Eq.~(\ref{eq:Doppler_DM ) as \begin{subequations \begin{equation}y^\mathrm{DM}_{re}(t_r) = \Re\left[\mathcal Y^\mathrm{DM}_{re}(t_r) e^{2\pi i q_0 t_r}\right] \, ,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\label{eq:mathcal_Y_DM} \mathcal Y^\mathrm{DM}_{re} = 2i\left(\hat n_{re} \cdot \hat e_v\right)\frac{\sqrt{16 \pi G \rho_\mathrm{DM}}v_\mathrm{DM}[Q_M]_d}{\omega_\phi c^2}\sin(\alpha^\mathrm{DM}_{re})e^{-i\beta^\mathrm{DM}_{re}}\, ,\end{equation} with \begin{equation}\beta^DM_{re} =\alpha^DM_{re}+2\pi q_0 t_r - \phi_r \,\nonumber = \alpha^DM_{re} - \Phi + 2 \pi (q_0-f_\phi) t_r + \frac{2\pi f_\phi}{c}\frac{|\vec v_DM|}{c}\hat k\cdot \vec x_r(t_r) \, . \label{eq:beta_DM}\end{equation} \end{subequations One can do the exact same job for the GW signals starting from Eq.~\eqref{eq:Doppler_GW to find \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\mathcal Y^GW_{re} = \frac{A}{2\left(1-\hat n_{re} \cdot \hat k_GW\right)}\sin(\alpha^GW_{re})\Re\left[i\hat h^SSB_{ij}(\imath,\Psi)\hat n^i_{re} \hat n^j_{re}e^{-i\beta^GW_{re}}\right]\,\label{eq:mathcal_Y} \alpha^GW_{re} = \pi\left(f_GW + \dot f_GW\xi_r\right)|\vec x_r(t_r)-\vec x_e(t_e)|\left(1-\hat k \cdot \hat n_{re}(t_r)\right) \, \label{eq:alpha} \beta^GW_{re} = \alpha^GW_{re} + \Phi_GW + 2 \pi (q_0-f_GW) t_r + \frac{2\pi f_GW}{c}\hat k\cdot \vec x_r(t_r) -\pi \dot f_GW\xi^2_r \, , \label{eq:beta}\end{equation} \end{subequations where $\xi_r=t_r-\hat k \cdot \vec x_r/c$.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-349: \begin{equation}\beta^DM_{re} =\alpha^DM_{re}+2\pi q_0 t_r - \phi_r \,\nonumber = \alpha^DM_{re} - \Phi + 2 \pi (q_0-f_\phi) t_r + \frac{2\pi f_\phi}{c}\frac{|\vec v_DM|}{c}\hat k\cdot \vec x_r(t_r) \, . \label{eq:beta_DM}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-350: \begin{equation}\mathcal Y^GW_{re} = \frac{A}{2\left(1-\hat n_{re} \cdot \hat k_GW\right)}\sin(\alpha^GW_{re})\Re\left[i\hat h^SSB_{ij}(\imath,\Psi)\hat n^i_{re} \hat n^j_{re}e^{-i\beta^GW_{re}}\right]\,\label{eq:mathcal_Y} \alpha^GW_{re} = \pi\left(f_GW + \dot f_GW\xi_r\right)|\vec x_r(t_r)-\vec x_e(t_e)|\left(1-\hat k \cdot \hat n_{re}(t_r)\right) \, \label{eq:alpha} \beta^GW_{re} = \alpha^GW_{re} + \Phi_GW + 2 \pi (q_0-f_GW) t_r + \frac{2\pi f_GW}{c}\hat k\cdot \vec x_r(t_r) -\pi \dot f_GW\xi^2_r \, , \label{eq:beta}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1533: We now quickly show how to express TDI combinations in this framework.
elements.sent-2411-1534: We will focus on the case of constant and equal arm-length $L$.
elements.sent-2411-1535: In TDI, we encounter quantities such as \begin{equation}y_{ij}\left(t-\frac{L}{c}\right) = Y_{ij}\left(t-\frac{L}{c}\right) e^{i\phi_i\left(t-\frac{L}{c}\right)}\, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1536: The quantity $Y_{ij}$ are slowly evolving with time such that $Y_{ij (t-L/c)\approx Y_{ij (t)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1537: For GW, this assumption holds if the GW amplitude slowly evolves with time, if the frequency drift of the GW is small and if the spacecraft velocity is small compared to the speed of light.
elements.sent-2411-1538: For DM, this assumption holds for the same reasons, in particular, the amplitude of the wave is constant (in the approximation where the time of integration is smaller than the coherence time), and $Y_{ij}$ depends on the spacecraft velocity, which is small compared to $c$.
elements.sent-2411-1539: Taking DM as an example, we can write \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\phi_i\left(t-\frac{L}{c}\right) =\omega_\phi t-\frac{\omega_\phi L}{c} -\vec k_\phi \cdot \vec x_i\left(t-\frac{L}{c}\right) +\Phi\, =\omega_\phi t-\frac{\omega_\phi L}{c} -\vec k_\phi \cdot \vec x_i(t) + \Phi + O(v_j/c \times L/x_i) \, \approx \phi_i(t) - \frac{\omega_\phi L}{c} \, ,\end{equation} \end{subequations where $v_j$ is the velocity of the emitting spacecraft.}}
elements.formula-2411-352: \begin{equation}\phi_i\left(t-\frac{L}{c}\right) =\omega_\phi t-\frac{\omega_\phi L}{c} -\vec k_\phi \cdot \vec x_i\left(t-\frac{L}{c}\right) +\Phi\, =\omega_\phi t-\frac{\omega_\phi L}{c} -\vec k_\phi \cdot \vec x_i(t) + \Phi + O(v_j/c \times L/x_i) \, \approx \phi_i(t) - \frac{\omega_\phi L}{c} \, ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1540: This leads to \begin{equation}y^DM_{ij}\left(t-\frac{L}{c}\right) \approx \Re\left[Y^DM_{ij}(t) e^{i\phi_i(t)}e^{-\frac{i\omega_\phi L}{c}}\right] =\Re\left[\mathcal Y^DM_{ij}(t)e^{-\frac{i\omega_\phi L}{c}} e^{2\pi i q_0 t}\right] \, .\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-353: \begin{equation}y^DM_{ij}\left(t-\frac{L}{c}\right) \approx \Re\left[Y^DM_{ij}(t) e^{i\phi_i(t)}e^{-\frac{i\omega_\phi L}{c}}\right] =\Re\left[\mathcal Y^DM_{ij}(t)e^{-\frac{i\omega_\phi L}{c}} e^{2\pi i q_0 t}\right] \, .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1541: Applying recursively this relationship leads to \begin{equation}D_{ijk}y^\mathrm{DM}_{kl}(t) = \Re\left[\mathcal Y^\mathrm{DM}_{kl}(t)e^{-\frac{2i\omega_\phi L}{c}} e^{2\pi i q_0 t}\right] \, ,\end{equation} and so on.
elements.formula-2411-354: \begin{equation}D_{ijk}y^DM_{kl}(t) = \Re\left[\mathcal Y^DM_{kl}(t)e^{-\frac{2i\omega_\phi L}{c}} e^{2\pi i q_0 t}\right] \, ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1542: Using this result, the first generation TDI combination can be written as \begin{subequations \begin{equation}X^\mathrm{DM}_1(t)=\Re\left[\mathcal X^\mathrm{DM}_1 (t) e^{2\pi i q_0 t}\right] \, ,\end{equation} and $\mathcal X^\mathrm{DM _1$ is slowly evolving with time and is given by (using Eq.~\eqref{eq:TDI_X_gen ) \begin{equation}\mathcal X^DM_1 = \left(1-e^{\frac{-2i\omega_\phi L}{c}} \right) \left[\mathcal Y^DM_{13} -\mathcal Y^DM_{12} + e^{\frac{-i\omega_\phi L}{c}}\left(\mathcal Y^DM_{31}-\mathcal Y^DM_{21}\right)\right] \nonumber = 2i \sin \left(\frac{\omega_\phi L}{c}\right)e^{\frac{-i\omega_\phi L}{c}} \left[\mathcal Y^DM_{13} -\mathcal Y^DM_{12} + e^{\frac{-i\omega_\phi L}{c}}\left(\mathcal Y^DM_{31}-\mathcal Y^DM_{21}\right)\right]\, .\label{eq:X1_sl}\end{equation} \end{subequations Similarly the second generation TDI can be written as \begin{subequations \begin{equation}X^\mathrm{DM}_2(t)=\Re\left[\mathcal X^\mathrm{DM}_2 (t) e^{2\pi i q_0 t}\right]\, ,\end{equation} with the slowly evolving part \begin{equation}\mathcal X^DM_2 = \left(1-e^{\frac{-2i\omega_\phi L}{c}}-e^{\frac{-4i\omega_\phi L}{c}}+e^{\frac{-6i\omega_\phi L}{c}}\right) \left[\mathcal Y^DM_{13} -\mathcal Y^DM_{12} + e^{\frac{-i\omega_\phi L}{c}}\left(\mathcal Y^DM_{31}-\mathcal Y^DM_{21}\right)\right]\, .\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-356: \begin{equation}\mathcal X^DM_1 = \left(1-e^{\frac{-2i\omega_\phi L}{c}} \right) \left[\mathcal Y^DM_{13} -\mathcal Y^DM_{12} + e^{\frac{-i\omega_\phi L}{c}}\left(\mathcal Y^DM_{31}-\mathcal Y^DM_{21}\right)\right] \nonumber = 2i \sin \left(\frac{\omega_\phi L}{c}\right)e^{\frac{-i\omega_\phi L}{c}} \left[\mathcal Y^DM_{13} -\mathcal Y^DM_{12} + e^{\frac{-i\omega_\phi L}{c}}\left(\mathcal Y^DM_{31}-\mathcal Y^DM_{21}\right)\right]\, .\label{eq:X1_sl}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-358: \begin{equation}\mathcal X^DM_2 = \left(1-e^{\frac{-2i\omega_\phi L}{c}}-e^{\frac{-4i\omega_\phi L}{c}}+e^{\frac{-6i\omega_\phi L}{c}}\right) \left[\mathcal Y^DM_{13} -\mathcal Y^DM_{12} + e^{\frac{-i\omega_\phi L}{c}}\left(\mathcal Y^DM_{31}-\mathcal Y^DM_{21}\right)\right]\, .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1543: Since $1-x-x^2+x^3=(1+x)(1-x)^2$, the previous expression becomes simply \begin{equation}\mathcal X^DM_2 = \left(1+e^{\frac{-2i\omega_\phi L}{c}}\right)\left(1-e^{\frac{-2i\omega_\phi L}{c}}\right)^2\left[\mathcal Y^DM_{13} -\mathcal Y^DM_{12} + e^{\frac{-i\omega_\phi L}{c}}\left(\mathcal Y^DM_{31}-\mathcal Y^DM_{21}\right)\right] \nonumber = \left(1+e^{\frac{-2i\omega_\phi L}{c}}\right)\left(1-e^{\frac{-2i\omega_\phi L}{c}}\right) \mathcal X^DM_1 =2 i \sin \left(\frac{2\omega_\phi L}{c}\right) e^{\frac{-2i\omega_\phi L}{c}}\mathcal X^DM_1\, . \label{eq:X2_sl}\end{equation} \end{subequations We have now expressed the second generation TDI combination in time domain induced by scalar DM as function of a slowly evolving part (the $\mathcal{X}$ factors) and a fast oscillating part, oscillating at the closest Fourier bin to the wave Compton frequency.}
elements.formula-2411-359: \begin{equation}\mathcal X^DM_2 = \left(1+e^{\frac{-2i\omega_\phi L}{c}}\right)\left(1-e^{\frac{-2i\omega_\phi L}{c}}\right)^2\left[\mathcal Y^DM_{13} -\mathcal Y^DM_{12} + e^{\frac{-i\omega_\phi L}{c}}\left(\mathcal Y^DM_{31}-\mathcal Y^DM_{21}\right)\right] \nonumber = \left(1+e^{\frac{-2i\omega_\phi L}{c}}\right)\left(1-e^{\frac{-2i\omega_\phi L}{c}}\right) \mathcal X^DM_1 =2 i \sin \left(\frac{2\omega_\phi L}{c}\right) e^{\frac{-2i\omega_\phi L}{c}}\mathcal X^DM_1\, . \label{eq:X2_sl}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1544: As mentioned previously, this formulation is very convenient because in Fourier domain, the signal is simply the product of the Fourier transform of $\mathcal{X}$, which is numerically fast to obtain, and the Fourier transform of the $\exp(2\pi i q_0 t)$, which has an analytical solution (see \cite{Cornish07 ).}
elements.sent-2411-1545: These expressions are directly implemented in FastGB and FastDM softwares for the fit of both GB and scalar ULDM data.
elements.sent-2411-1546: Propagation of the electric field from the dish to the fictional plane in SHUKET The calculation presented in Section ~\ref{sec:propag_field is based on the thin optical element approximation, which is valid only for low curvature dish $R\gg r$, see the discussion in Section ~\ref{sec:dish_plane .}}
elements.sent-2411-1547: In this appendix, we want to check if Eq.~\eqref{Vinet is valid in the SHUKET setup, in the case of emission from standing DP electric field.}
elements.sent-2411-1548: To do so, we propose the following "reverse engineering-like" method :.
elements.sent-2411-1549: Consider the field on the dish using boundary conditions Eq.~\eqref{eq:E_out , i.e $\vec U_D(\vec x) = i\chi \omega_U \vec Y_{\parallel, D (\vec x)$,.}}
elements.sent-2411-1550: Compute the field at each point $\vec x'$ on the plane closing the dish $\vec U_P(\vec x')$ from Eq.~\eqref{Vinet ,.}
elements.sent-2411-1551: Compute numerically the field on the dish $\vec U^\mathrm{test _D(\vec x)$ from $\vec U_P(\vec x')$ using Kirchhoff integral theorem Eq.~\eqref{E_Dirichlet ,.}}
elements.sent-2411-1552: Compute the relative error between $\vec U_D(\vec x) $ and $\vec U^\mathrm{test _D(\vec x)$.}
elements.sent-2411-1553: We consider the plane located at $z'=R-a$, then the field at a point $(\rho', \phi',z')$ on the plane going towards the dish, located at $z \geq z'$, is given by \begin{equation}\vec U_P\rightarrow D(\rho',\phi',z') = i\chi \omega_U e^{-ikf(\rho')} \vec Y_{\parallel, D}(\rho',\phi',f(\rho')+z') \, . \label{E_plane_Vinet}\end{equation} Notice the change of sign on the wavevector $k$, compared to Eq.~\eqref{Vinet as we are now considering emission towards the dish (positive $z$ axis).}
elements.formula-2411-360: \begin{equation}\vec U_P\rightarrow D(\rho',\phi',z') = i\chi \omega_U e^{-ikf(\rho')} \vec Y_{\parallel, D}(\rho',\phi',f(\rho')+z') \, . \label{E_plane_Vinet}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1554: In addition, we aim at computing the field reflected by the dish, while Eq.~\eqref{Vinet gives the incident field.}
elements.sent-2411-1555: By boundary conditions, we assume that both incident and reflected are equal, up to a sign, hence the positive sign in front of Eq.\eqref{E_plane_Vinet .}
elements.sent-2411-1556: Using Eqs.~\eqref{E_Dirichlet , \eqref{Green_plane and \eqref{E_plane_Vinet , the field in a point on the receiving surface, i.e, the dish, of coordinates $(\rho,\phi,z)$ is given by \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\vec U^test_D(\rho,\phi,f(\rho)+z') = -\frac{i\chi \omega_U f(\rho)}{2\pi}\int_0^r d\rho' \rho'e^{-ikf(\rho')}\int_0^{2\pi}d\phi' \frac{ikL-1}{L^3}e^{ikL}\vec Y_{\parallel, D}(\rho',\phi', f(\rho')+z') \, \approx -\frac{i\chi \omega_U f(\rho)}{2\pi} Y_x\, Y_y\, 0 \int_0^r d\rho' \rho'e^{-ikf(\rho')}\int_0^{2\pi} d\phi' \frac{ikL-1}{L^3}e^{ikL} \, ,\end{equation} \end{subequations where the difference of $z$ positions $\Delta z$ between the fictional plane and the dish from Eq.~\eqref{Green_plane is now positive and corresponds exactly to $f(\rho)$, $\Delta z=f(\rho) = (r^2-\rho^2)/2R > 0$.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-361: \begin{equation}\vec U^test_D(\rho,\phi,f(\rho)+z') = -\frac{i\chi \omega_U f(\rho)}{2\pi}\int_0^r d\rho' \rho'e^{-ikf(\rho')}\int_0^{2\pi}d\phi' \frac{ikL-1}{L^3}e^{ikL}\vec Y_{\parallel, D}(\rho',\phi', f(\rho')+z') \, \approx -\frac{i\chi \omega_U f(\rho)}{2\pi} Y_x\, Y_y\, 0 \int_0^r d\rho' \rho'e^{-ikf(\rho')}\int_0^{2\pi} d\phi' \frac{ikL-1}{L^3}e^{ikL} \, ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1557: Then, the relative error between $\vec U^\mathrm{test _D$ and $\vec U_D \approx i \chi \omega_U \left(Y_x,Y_y,0\right)^T$ (from Eq.~\eqref{field_polarization ) is \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\label{error_Vinet} \epsilon(\rho,\phi) \approx \left|\frac{\vec U^test_D - \vec U_D}{\vec U_D}\right|\approx \left|-\frac{f(\rho)}{2\pi}\left(\int_0^r d\rho' \rho'e^{-ikf(\rho')}\int_0^{2\pi} d\phi' \frac{ikL-1}{L^3}e^{ikL}\right) - 1 \right| \, .\end{equation}. \end{subequations.}}}}
elements.figure-2411-030: \begin{minipage}{\textwidth} \begin{minipage}{0.65\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{images/epsilon_3.pdf} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.32\textwidth} \caption{Numerical computation of $\epsilon(\rho,\phi)$ from Eq.~\eqref{error_Vinet} for various dish positions $(\rho,\phi)$. Over the full surface, it is of the order of the \textperthousand. The dish antenna is located inside the non-radiative near field region of the fictional plane (i.e the distance $L<0.62 \sqrt{(2r)^3/\lambda}$). One can notice the interference pattern with a typical period $\sim 0.05$ m, which corresponds to the wavelength of emission.} \label{fig:err_relat_Vinet} \end{minipage} \end{minipage}
elements.sent-2411-1558: Fig.~\ref{fig:err_relat_Vinet shows the numerical computation of this relative error as function of the position $(\rho, \phi)$ on the dish.}
elements.sent-2411-1559: One can notice that the error is less than 1\%, implying that Eq.~\eqref{Vinet can be safely used for the propagation of the electric field from the dish in the SHUKET system, with negligible error.}
elements.sent-2411-1560: Note that the error $\epsilon(\rho,\phi)$ is computed only for points on the dish with radial coordinates $\rho < r$, as the circle of points belonging to the dish with coordinates $(r,\phi,z_\mathrm{plane ), \phi \in [0,2\pi[$ belongs to the fictional plane as well, and Kirchhoff integral is only valid for the computation of the field at reception points outside the emission surface.}
elements.sent-2411-1561: Therefore, one can deduce that the calculations presented in \cite{Vinet are valid for SHUKET.}
